Illustrated SGNN
Contents Paper GitHub Open in Colab ↗

Implementation guide · Mechanistic pretraining · PyTorch · Canadian PHAC data

From Simulated Epidemics to Real Forecasts

Mathematical biologists, epidemiologists, and public health researchers have spent decades building mechanistic models of how diseases spread. That work is good. The problem is that it runs into a wall when you try to combine it with deep learning: neural networks need far more outbreak data than any single epidemic produces, and the data that does exist arrives delayed, under-reported, and revised weekly. During COVID-19, the only way around this was to train forecasting models on the very outbreak they were trying to predict — not a methodological failure, just the only option available under a novel pathogen with no historical precedent. It worked, carefully. But it left a real question open: can a neural network learn to forecast epidemics without ever seeing real outbreak data? A 2025 paper by Dudley et al. answered yes. They generated 15,000 synthetic epidemics from a mathematical model, trained a neural network on those, and deployed it cold on real US mortality data without any fine-tuning. It outperformed the average CDC ensemble model by nearly 3×. This tutorial takes that framework and applies it to Canadian PHAC surveillance data — an extension the paper itself did not attempt. Whether the approach transfers, and where it struggles, is what the rest of this walkthrough shows.

✓ All levels welcome 10 illustrated sections 20 runnable cells PyTorch + PHAC data
Run on Colab Read the Paper ↗
True infections vs. reported surveillance

PyTorch primers

Official NN tutorial ↗ Sahin: beginner walkthrough ↗

If you are new to the method, start with the paper explanation — it covers the idea without equations. The code sections follow in the same order. Each figure comes before the code it explains, not after.

📚 New to PyTorch? Start here first

This blog assumes basic Python familiarity, but zero deep-learning experience. If PyTorch is completely new to you, spend 20 minutes with these two guides before continuing — they cover exactly the concepts used throughout this code:

  • PyTorch Official Tutorial — Neural Networks: defines nn.Module, the forward pass, loss functions, backprop, and the optimizer step. Every one of these appears in the code below.
  • Sahin Ahmed — Train a Neural Network in PyTorch: a friendly walkthrough that builds intuition for training loops, DataLoaders, and evaluation — in the same step-by-step style used here.

Come back when you can answer: What does loss.backward() do? What does optimizer.step() do? Those two lines are the heart of every training loop.

Replicate the results: 5 steps
  1. Open the Colab notebook — click here. Set Runtime → T4 GPU for best speed.
  2. Run Cell 1 to import libraries and fix seeds. You should see Device: cuda.
  3. Run Cells 2–3 to download PHAC data and plot the sanity check. Confirm you see five COVID waves.
  4. Run Cells 4–11 to define the simulator and generate training data. ⌚ ~12 min on T4
  5. Run Cells 12–19 to train EpiCNN and produce rolling forecasts for Canada + all 10 provinces. ⌚ ~5 min on T4
Total on free Colab T4: ~18 minutes  ·  CPU only: set N_TRAIN=5000, N_EPOCHS=3 → ~45 min

Section 00The big picture — start here

Start with the whole system as a black box. You give it 26 weeks of real Canadian case counts. It gives back an 8-week forecast with a 90% confidence band. That is it from the outside.

Fig 0.1 — the SGNN as a black box
The SGNN from the outside: real weekly case counts in, probabilistic 8-week forecast out. The model was trained on zero real data.

Now open the box. Inside are four stages that depend on each other. Skip any one and the model stops working.

Fig 0.2 — opening the black box: the four-stage pipeline
Steps 1–3 run entirely on simulated data and repeat 15,000 times with different random parameters. Step 4 applies the finished model cold to real Canadian surveillance data — no adjustments at all.
The core insight

Real epidemics obey the same mathematical laws as simulated ones. If the simulator is diverse enough, a network trained on fake epidemics will recognise real ones — just like a pilot trained in a simulator can land a real plane the first time they fly one.

The PaperSimulation as Supervision — the idea, gently explained

The paper behind this work is "Simulation as Supervision: Mechanistic Pretraining for Scientific Discovery" by Dudley, Magdaleno, Harding, and Eisenberg (2025, arXiv:2507.08977). It proposes a framework called Simulation-Grounded Neural Networks (SGNNs).

The core idea is simple enough to fit in a sentence, but the reasoning behind it takes a bit more unpacking. Five ideas, each building on the last.

Paper overview — five ideas that build on each other
IDEA 1 — THE PROBLEM
Science has rich theoretical models and rich data, but combining them properly is genuinely hard. Mechanistic models are rigid; data-driven models need data that pandemics rarely provide in quantity.
↓
IDEA 2 — WHY EXISTING FIXES FALL SHORT
Physics-constrained ML (PINNs) enforces theory too rigidly — if the equations are even slightly wrong, the predictions are wrong too. Pure data-driven ML ignores theory entirely and needs far more data than a single pandemic provides. Both fail when real data is scarce and equations are imperfect.
↓
IDEA 3 — THE SGNN INSIGHT ★
Do not constrain the network with equations — use the equations as a data factory instead. Run the mechanistic model thousands of times with randomised parameters to generate a huge synthetic training set. Train a neural network on that set. Deploy on real data with no further updates. The network learns the underlying dynamics from simulations, so real epidemic curves feel familiar even though it has never seen one.
↓
IDEA 4 — WHAT IT ACHIEVES
Nearly 3× better COVID-19 mortality forecasting skill than the average CDC ensemble model — with zero real training data. The approach holds up even when the simulator assumptions are wrong, and generalises across four scientific disciplines: epidemiology, ecology, social science, and chemistry.
↓
IDEA 5 — THIS BLOG
Full implementation on Canadian COVID-19 data, end to end — every compartment, every noise layer, every training loop, every forecast. The Canadian data is the worked example; the framework applies to any disease in any country.
The five ideas stack: each one motivates the next. Idea 3 is the paper's core contribution. The code that follows implements it on Canadian COVID-19 data.

P.1Idea 1: the fundamental tension in science

Epidemiologists have equations for how diseases spread. They also have surveillance data. The problem is that these two things are surprisingly hard to combine well. The equations are too rigid — they assume the world behaves a certain way, and when it does not, they break. The data is too sparse — a single pandemic gives you maybe 200 weekly observations, which is not enough to train a neural network from scratch.

So you are stuck. Use the equations and you get a model that cracks under real-world complexity. Use a neural network and you do not have enough data to train it properly. The paper is about escaping that trap.

Pure mechanistic model

Write differential equations. Fit parameters to data. Very interpretable — every term has a biological meaning. But rigid: if the equations are wrong, the predictions are wrong. And COVID changed every assumption, every few months.

Pure data-driven neural net

Train a network on historical data. Very flexible — it learns whatever patterns exist. But needs a lot of data. COVID happened once. A few hundred weekly data points per province is nowhere near enough to train a neural network from scratch.

P.2Idea 2: why hybrid approaches still fall short

Two main camps have tried combining theory and data. Neither works when real data is scarce and equations are imperfect:

Approach A — Physics-constrained ML (PINN-style)

Add a penalty term to the loss that pushes the network toward the differential equations. Sounds sensible. The problem is that epidemic models are never exactly right. COVID introduced asymptomatic spread, waning immunity, and five overlapping variant waves — none of which fit cleanly into a SEIR model. When the equations are wrong and you constrain the network to obey them anyway, you have built bias directly into your learning objective.

Approach B — Neural ODE / hybrid models

Embed the equations inside the network architecture. More flexible than A — the network can bend around the equations rather than being hard-constrained by them. But you still need to commit to a fixed equation structure upfront. When the real outbreak keeps surprising you (Delta, then Omicron, then BA.2), a fixed structure keeps failing in new ways.

Both treat the equations as a constraint. The SGNN paper uses them as a source of training data instead.

P.3Idea 3: the core insight — simulation as supervision

The SGNN insight, stated plainly:

The SGNN insight

Use the equations as a data factory, not a constraint. Run the mechanistic model thousands of times with randomised parameters. Train a neural network on the synthetic output. Then deploy it on real data — no updates, no fine-tuning. The network has seen enough fake epidemics to recognise the real thing when it shows up.

Fig P.1 — SGNN vs. previous approaches: where the equations live
The difference is where the equations live. In mechanistic models they are the prediction engine. In physics-constrained ML they are a loss penalty. In SGNNs they are a data factory — which means the network can generalise even when the equations are imperfect.

P.4The observation model: a detail that makes everything work

One thing the paper gets right that most approaches miss: you cannot simulate clean epidemic curves and train on those. Real surveillance data is never clean. It is delayed, under-counted, biased by day of week, distorted by lab batch errors.

There is one problem with training on synthetic epidemic curves: they are too clean. Real surveillance data arrives delayed, under-counted, and distorted by weekends. If you train on smooth theoretical curves and then test on real PHAC data, the model has never seen anything like what it is looking at. The paper fixes this with an observation model — a pipeline that takes the clean synthetic curve and deliberately degrades it to look like real surveillance. The network trains on the degraded version and never sees the true underlying signal.

The observation model as domain randomisation

This is a form of domain randomisation borrowed from robotics simulation-to-reality transfer. By training on a distribution of noisy versions of the true signal, the model learns to handle real surveillance noise even without knowing its exact properties in advance. The four layers capture the dominant sources of bias in real infectious disease reporting:

  • Under-reporting: the largest bias. In early COVID-19, testing capacity was near zero; true infections may have been 10–20× reported cases. The logistic ramp captures the testing scale-up trajectory seen across Canada.
  • Day-of-week effect: ubiquitous. Labs process fewer samples on weekends; reporting pipelines batch-process Monday morning. The pattern is clearly visible in raw PHAC downloads.
  • Lab batch noise: the ±8% multiplicative noise and rare bad-batch failures capture real PCR batch-to-batch efficiency variation. Occasional failed batches produce the visible dips seen in surveillance series.
  • Reporting delays: cases diagnosed today do not appear in the feed for days. The delay shrinks as lab infrastructure scales up — exactly the logistic curve used here.
Why does adding noise matter so much?

Because at test time the model sees real PHAC data, which is messy in very specific ways: cases under-reported early in the pandemic, a dip every weekend, occasional bad lab batches. If you trained on perfectly clean epidemic curves, the model would get to real data and effectively see a completely different distribution. It would not know what to do with it. Training on deliberately degraded synthetic data means the model has already learned to work with messy, biased, delayed signals. Nothing in the real data surprises it.

P.5What the paper found — the results

The results, honestly, are better than you might expect from a model trained on no real data:

Fig P.2 — key results from the paper (Dudley et al., 2025)
The most striking result: nearly 3× better COVID-19 mortality forecasting skill than the average CDC ensemble model — with zero real training data. SGNNs also held up when trained on simulations with incorrect biological assumptions — something physics-constrained approaches fail at.

P.6What "zero-shot" actually means in this context

"Zero-shot" gets overloaded in ML. Here it means something specific and verifiable:

1

Training: entirely synthetic

The model trains on 15,000 randomly generated fake epidemics. No real COVID-19 case counts are used. Not even indirectly — synthetic val split only.

2

Deployment: real data, frozen weights

The trained model is applied directly to real PHAC surveillance data. The weights are not updated. No fine-tuning. No gradient steps on real data.

3

Transfer: log-normalisation is the bridge

The only thing connecting training and deployment is the normalisation: log1p(series) / max. This maps the real weekly case counts into the same [0, 1] range the model trained in. That is the entire transfer mechanism.

4

Evaluation: rolling MAE on real PHAC data

Performance is measured by comparing the model's 8-week forecasts to the actual PHAC case counts that were released later. This is a genuine out-of-sample evaluation on data the model never saw in any form.

P.7Why this matters beyond COVID-19

The same trick works anywhere you have equations but not much data — which covers more of science than you might think:

DomainThe equationsThe data problemSGNN approach
Epidemic forecastingSEIR compartment modelsOne pandemic, noisy surveillanceSimulate 15,000 epidemics
EcologyLotka–Volterra predator-preySpecies counts are expensive to collectSimulate ecosystem trajectories
ChemistryReaction kinetics equationsLab experiments are slow and costlySimulate reaction pathways
ClimateGCM differential equationsOnly one Earth, one historySimulate climate scenarios
Your domainWhatever equations describe your systemLimited observationsRun them 10,000+ times with randomised params
✈ The flight simulator analogy

Boeing does not teach pilots by crashing planes. They build a simulator that encodes the physics, randomise the scenarios, and let pilots accumulate thousands of hours in fake conditions. By the time a pilot lands a real aircraft, the physics feel familiar even if that specific runway does not. The SGNN approach does the same thing for a neural network: train on physics-generated fake epidemics so that real outbreak dynamics feel like something the model has seen before.

P.8How to apply this to your own data — the mental checklist

Before writing a single line of code, answer these four questions. They determine every configurable part of the pipeline, and getting them wrong early is much more expensive than getting them wrong late.

✓ Checklist: applying SGNN to a new problem
  • Do you have equations? Any domain-specific mathematical model of the dynamics (not necessarily perfect — approximate is fine). If yes, proceed.
  • What are the plausible ranges for each parameter? You will sample these randomly. Broad ranges = more diverse training data = better generalisation.
  • What does "observation" look like in your domain? How does the true signal get distorted before you see it? Build an observation model that mimics those distortions.
  • What is your input window and forecast horizon? How much history does your forecaster see, and how far ahead does it predict? These become T_IN and T_OUT.

For Canadian COVID-19 data: SEAIR equations (standard), parameters from the epidemiology literature, an observation model that mimics PHAC surveillance noise, and 26 weeks of history to predict 8 weeks out. That is all the domain knowledge required. The rest is PyTorch.

That is all you need to know before the code starts. Everything from here builds exactly that.

📖 Glossary — terms used throughout

Two audiences read this: epidemiologists who know R0 but not backpropagation, and ML practitioners who know loss functions but not compartmental models. This glossary covers both.

TermPlain meaning
BatchA subset of training data processed together before updating model weights. Batch size 512 means 512 sliding windows go through the network before each weight update.
Compartmental modelAn epidemic model that divides the population into groups (S, E, I, R) and tracks daily flows between them. SEAIR is a compartmental model.
Domain randomisationTraining on a wide distribution of synthetic scenarios so the model generalises to real conditions it was never directly trained on. The observation model here uses it by sampling different noise levels and delays per run.
EpochOne full pass through the training dataset. Ten epochs means each training window is seen ten times total.
Force of infectionThe daily probability that a susceptible person becomes infected, given current prevalence and transmission rate. In SEAIR: FOI = β × (I + fa×A) / N.
Log-normalisationApplying log1p(x) / max to a time series. Maps any epidemic curve to [0, 1] by shape, regardless of absolute scale. Ontario (500k/wk peak) and Newfoundland (8k/wk peak) both map to 1.0 at their own peaks when normalised per-province.
Observation modelA pipeline that degrades clean simulator output to look like real surveillance data. Applies under-reporting, day-of-week bias, lab batch noise, and reporting delays.
Pinball lossA loss function for quantile predictions. At quantile α, it penalises over-predictions α times more than under-predictions, pushing the output to the correct percentile of the distribution.
R0Basic reproduction number. The average number of secondary infections from one case in a fully susceptible population. R0 > 1: epidemic grows. R0 < 1: it dies out. R0 ≈ β / γ in a simple SIR model.
Rolling forecastGenerating forecasts at consecutive time points by sliding the input window forward. At each step the model sees the most recent T_IN weeks and predicts the next T_OUT weeks.
Sliding windowCreating multiple training examples from one time series by sliding a window of width T_IN along it, generating an (input, label) pair at each position.
Stochastic simulationA simulation where random draws determine outcomes at each step. Two runs with identical parameters produce different trajectories. Contrast with deterministic simulation, where the same parameters always give the same result.
Zero-shotApplying a trained model to a new dataset without any additional training on it. Here: the model trains on synthetic data and runs on real PHAC case counts with frozen weights.

Section 01From paper to code — the implementation plan

Every concept from the paper has a direct code counterpart. The table maps them.

Fig 1.1 — paper concepts mapped to implementation modules
Every paper concept has a direct code counterpart. Adapting to a new domain means replacing Rows 1–2 with domain-specific equations and an observation model. The training loop, network, and deployment code stay identical.
📌 Adapting to your own data — the minimal change set

If you are applying SGNN to a different disease, country, or domain, you only need to change two things: (1) HumanToHumanSimulator — replace or retune the parameters to match your population and disease; and (2) canada_cfg — update the config for your target population size, expected wave structure, and NPI patterns. The EpiCNN architecture, training loop, and rolling forecast function stay identical.

Section 02Setup & Canadian COVID-19 data

🚀 Google Colab setup

A free T4 GPU at colab.research.google.com runs the full 15,000-trajectory pipeline in 10–15 minutes. On CPU only, set N_TRAIN = 5000 and N_EPOCHS = 3 to finish in under an hour.

2.1Install dependencies

All required packages are pre-installed on Colab. If running locally, install with: pip install torch numpy pandas matplotlib tqdm

Cell 1 — imports & reproducibility seeds
import enum, random, math, os, warnings
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict, Optional, Tuple, Any
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker
import matplotlib.dates as mdates
from tqdm.auto import tqdm

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset

warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Fix random seeds for reproducibility
SEED = 42
random.seed(SEED); np.random.seed(SEED); torch.manual_seed(SEED)

DEVICE = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'
print(f'Device: {DEVICE}  |  PyTorch: {torch.__version__}')
# Expected: Device: cuda  |  PyTorch: 2.x.x+cu121  (on Colab T4)
💡 What these lines do

SEED = 42 — fixes the random number generators so you get the same results every time you run. Without this, each run produces slightly different epidemic curves and slightly different model weights. DEVICE = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu' — detects whether a GPU is available. On Colab with a T4, this returns 'cuda', which makes training ~10x faster than CPU.

2.2Download PHAC surveillance data

The Public Health Agency of Canada publishes weekly COVID-19 surveillance data at a stable public URL. The key columns:

ColumnWhat it isHow we use it
pruidProvince numeric code1 = national; 35 = Ontario; 59 = BC…
dateWeek-ending dateX-axis of every plot
numtotal_last7New reported cases in last 7 daysThe series we forecast — our target Y
numdeaths_last7New deaths in last 7 daysOptional extension (see Section 10)
💻 Package versions — check before running

All code was tested on the stack below. On Colab everything is pre-installed. Running locally, install with pip install torch numpy pandas matplotlib tqdm.

PackageTested versionNote
Python3.10+3.9 works; 3.8 is untested
PyTorch2.1+CPU works; GPU cuts training from ~8 min to ~90 sec
NumPy1.24+ 
pandas2.0+ 
matplotlib3.7+ 
tqdm4.65+progress bars during simulation
 
Version check — paste into a cell and run first
import sys, torch, numpy, pandas, matplotlib
print(f"Python:     {sys.version.split()[0]}")
print(f"PyTorch:    {torch.__version__}")
print(f"NumPy:      {numpy.__version__}")
print(f"pandas:     {pandas.__version__}")
print(f"matplotlib: {matplotlib.__version__}")
# Expected output (approximately):
# Python:     3.10.x
# PyTorch:    2.1.x+cu118
# NumPy:      1.24.x
# pandas:     2.0.x
# matplotlib: 3.7.x
Cell 2 — download & parse PHAC dataset
COVID_URL = 'https://health-infobase.canada.ca/src/data/covidLive/covid19-download.csv'

print('Downloading Canadian COVID-19 data...')
df_raw = pd.read_csv(COVID_URL)
df_raw['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_raw['date'], dayfirst=True, errors='coerce')

# National series (pruid == 1)
canada = (df_raw[df_raw['pruid'] == 1]
          .sort_values('date').reset_index(drop=True))
canada['weekly_cases']  = canada['numtotal_last7'].fillna(0).clip(lower=0)
canada['weekly_deaths'] = canada['numdeaths_last7'].fillna(0).clip(lower=0)

print(f'Date range: {canada.date.min().date()} to {canada.date.max().date()}')
print(f'Weeks of data: {len(canada)}')
print(f'Peak weekly cases: {canada.weekly_cases.max():,.0f}')

# All 10 provincial series (used for zero-shot evaluation in Section 9)
PROV_CODES = {
    10: 'Newfoundland', 12: 'Nova Scotia',   13: 'New Brunswick',
    24: 'Quebec',       35: 'Ontario',       46: 'Manitoba',      47: 'Saskatchewan',
    48: 'Alberta',      59: 'British Columbia',
}
prov_series = {}
for code, name in PROV_CODES.items():
    sub = (df_raw[df_raw['pruid'] == code].sort_values('date').reset_index(drop=True))
    sub['weekly_cases'] = sub['numtotal_last7'].fillna(0).clip(lower=0)
    prov_series[name] = sub['weekly_cases'].values.astype(float)

canada_obs = canada['weekly_cases'].values.astype(float)
dates      = canada['date'].values
N_WEEKS    = len(canada_obs)
print(f'N_WEEKS = {N_WEEKS}')   # typically 180-200 depending on download date
What each line does — Cell 2 breakdown
  • pd.read_csv(COVID_URL) — downloads the entire PHAC surveillance CSV directly into a DataFrame. No manual download needed. If this fails, check your internet connection or the PHAC URL has changed.
  • pd.to_datetime(..., dayfirst=True) — converts the date strings (which PHAC writes as DD/MM/YYYY) into proper Python datetime objects. Without dayfirst=True, January 3rd would be parsed as March 1st.
  • df_raw[df_raw['pruid'] == 1] — filters to the national total row only. Every province has its own rows; pruid=1 is Canada as a whole.
  • .fillna(0).clip(lower=0) — replaces missing weeks with zero (PHAC has some gaps early in the pandemic) and clips negative values (which occasionally appear due to data corrections).
  • prov_series dictionary — stores each province's weekly case array by name. Sections 08 and 09 loop over this to produce provincial forecasts without any code changes.
Cell 3 — sanity plot: cases & deaths over time
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(14, 6), sharex=True)
axes[0].fill_between(dates, canada['weekly_cases'], alpha=0.45, color='steelblue')
axes[0].plot(dates, canada['weekly_cases'], color='steelblue', lw=0.9)
axes[0].set_title('Canada — Weekly New COVID-19 Cases (PHAC)', fontsize=12)
axes[0].set_ylabel('Weekly cases')
axes[0].yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(
    lambda x, _: f'{x/1000:.0f}k' if x >= 1000 else f'{x:.0f}'))

axes[1].fill_between(dates, canada['weekly_deaths'], alpha=0.45, color='salmon')
axes[1].plot(dates, canada['weekly_deaths'], color='salmon', lw=0.9)
axes[1].set_title('Canada — Weekly New COVID-19 Deaths (PHAC)', fontsize=12)
axes[1].set_ylabel('Weekly deaths')
axes[1].xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
axes[1].xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator(bymonth=[1, 4, 7, 10]))
plt.xticks(rotation=35); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
What to look for in the data plot
  • Wave heights vary dramatically. Omicron BA.1 (early 2022) peaks ~15× higher than the 2020 first wave. This is why the simulator uses piecewise-β to produce different peak sizes.
  • Deaths lag cases by 2–3 weeks. That lag gets smaller as testing improved. The observation model's delay layer reproduces this.
  • The series never hits zero. An endemic baseline stays noisy throughout. The importation parameter in the simulator keeps this floor alive.
  • Weekend dips are visible. Counts drop every Saturday–Sunday because labs and clinics reduce capacity. The day-of-week layer in the observation model produces exactly this pattern.

Section 03The epidemic simulator — the data factory

This is the most important section. The simulator is what makes zero-shot transfer possible: it manufactures all the training data from scratch.

Why do we need a simulator at all?

We need training pairs: (epidemic history, future epidemic). We have almost none from real life. So we manufacture them. Run a mathematical model of disease spread, get a fake epidemic curve, use the first 26 weeks as input and the next 8 weeks as label. Repeat 15,000 times with different parameters.

3.1The compartment model — a picture first

SEAIR is a compartmental model. Every person in the simulated population sits in exactly one of five compartments at any moment, and moves between them based on daily probability draws. The model is stochastic — two runs with the same parameters produce different trajectories, which is exactly what you want when generating training diversity.

The five compartments:

  1. S — Susceptible. No immunity yet. Anyone here can be infected if they contact someone infectious. Canada starts with ~38M here (minus however many start already recovered, which varies per run).
  2. E — Exposed. Infected but not yet contagious. The incubation period sits here. Rate out = σ (the config samples σ between 0.18–0.35/day, giving incubation periods of 3–6 days — consistent with COVID-19's observed range).
  3. A — Asymptomatic infectious. Contagious but never develop symptoms. Estimates range from 20–45% of COVID-19 infections. They transmit at roughly 50–75% the rate of symptomatic individuals (captured by asymp_trans_factor). In surveillance data, the vast majority are never counted — a key driver of under-reporting.
  4. I — Symptomatic infectious. Contagious and symptomatic. These are the cases that show up in PHAC reports (after the observation model's under-reporting discount). Rate out = γ (1/infectious_days). For COVID-19, the infectious period is typically 5–10 days, giving γ ≈ 0.10–0.20/day. The config samples this range.
  5. R — Recovered / immune. No longer infectious. With waning immunity enabled (90% of runs), some fraction returns to S each week — this is the R→S arrow that causes second and third waves.
Fig 3.1 — SEAIR compartments with waning immunity
The dashed amber arc (R→S) is what separates a one-wave model from a realistic multi-wave one. Without ω, the simulator only ever produces a single bell-shaped outbreak.
SymbolNamePlain EnglishRange
βTransmission rateProbability an S-I contact causes infection. Higher = faster spread.0.18–0.32
γRecovery rateFraction of infectious people recovering daily. 1/γ ≈ mean infectious days.0.07–0.20
σIncubation rateHow fast E→I. 1/σ ≈ mean incubation period in days.0.18–0.35
ωWaning rateHow fast R→S (immunity fades). 1/ω ≈ months of protection.0.001–0.006
💧 Analogy: leaky water tanks

β is the pipe from S to E. γ drains I and A into R. ω pokes a slow hole in R and lets water trickle back into S — that is waning immunity. The simulator runs this water-flow system forward 2,000 days using random Binomial draws at each step rather than smooth continuous flow. That randomness makes every run unique.

3.2Six ingredients that make runs diverse enough

1

Multiple waves (piecewise β)

β changes at 3–5 random time-points within each run. Without this, every trajectory is a single symmetric bell curve. With it, you get Alpha/Delta/Omicron-style multi-wave patterns.

2

Seasonality harmonics

Random cosine terms multiply β each day, producing winter surges. Respiratory viruses typically spread 20–40% faster in cold weather.

3

NPI policy — simulated lockdowns

When cases cross a random threshold, β drops. When they stay low long enough, it returns. Every lockdown profile is randomised, so the model trains on many different intervention shapes.

4

Super-spreading events

0.05–2% of infectious people drive outsized bursts. Gamma-distributed multipliers produce the explosive early-growth tails seen in real COVID outbreaks.

5

Demographics: births, deaths, importation

Poisson births add S individuals daily. Binomial deaths remove from each compartment. Importation seeds new cases from outside, keeping the epidemic alive between waves and maintaining an endemic floor.

6

Random initial immunity fraction

Each run starts with a random fraction already in R, mimicking the pre-existing partial immunity real populations had before each new Canadian wave.

Fig 3.2 — six sample simulator runs, different seeds, same Canada config
Six runs, same Canada config, different seeds. Each shows a distinct epidemic shape — a sharp single peak (seed 4), two clearly separated waves (seed 5), three waves (seed 2), four waves (seed 1), an NPI-flattened plateau where transmission drops mid-outbreak (seed 315), and a seasonal endemic pattern with five discernible peaks (seed 37). The model trains on all of these.

3.3Parameter reference

Every epidemiological quantity in the simulator maps to a named field in the config. The table below gives the symbol, what it controls biologically, the range the config samples from, and the published COVID-19 literature range for comparison. Use this when adapting the simulator to a different disease.

SymbolConfig field(s)Biological meaningConfig rangeCOVID-19 literature
β beta_min / beta_max Daily transmission rate per contact. Higher = faster spread. R0 ≈ β/γ. 0.18–0.32 0.20–0.35 (original); up to 0.80+ (Omicron)
γ gamma_min / gamma_max Daily recovery rate. 1/γ = mean infectious period in days. 0.07–0.20 0.07–0.20 (5–14 day infectious period)
σ sigma_min / sigma_max Rate of progression from exposed to infectious. 1/σ = mean incubation period. 0.18–0.35 0.17–0.33 (3–6 day incubation for COVID-19)
ω omega_min / omega_max Waning immunity rate. 1/ω = mean time from recovery to susceptibility again. Drives second and third waves. 0.001–0.006 0.002–0.007 (immunity lasting 4–18 months)
R0 derived: β/γ Basic reproduction number. Number of secondary infections from one case in a fully susceptible population. ~0.9–4.6 (sampled implicitly) 2.5–3.5 (original); 8–15 (Omicron)
pa asymp_prob_beta Probability that a new infection is asymptomatic. Beta-distributed. ~0.20–0.60 (Beta(3,7) prior) 20–45% of infections (seroprevalence-adjusted)
fa asymp_trans_beta Relative transmission rate of asymptomatics vs. symptomatics. ~0.20–0.70 (Beta(2,5) prior) 50–75% of symptomatic rate
A seasonality_amp_min / max Amplitude of the seasonal cosine multiplier on β. At A=0.3, winter transmission is 30% above the annual mean. 0.15–0.40 ~0.20–0.40 for respiratory viruses in temperate climates
N pop_size_min / max Population size. Determines absolute case counts; log-normalisation makes the model scale-invariant across different N values. Canada: fixed at 38M Canada population (2020): ~37.7M
Adapting for a different disease

R0 = β/γ is the first number to calibrate. Look up the published R0 for your target pathogen, pick a γ that matches the known infectious period, and compute β = R0 × γ. Then widen the range by ±30% to cover variant uncertainty. Influenza: R0 ≈ 1.2–1.4, infectious period 3–5 days, so γ ≈ 0.20–0.33 and β ≈ 0.24–0.46. Measles: R0 ≈ 12–18 — you would need to raise beta_max substantially.

3.4Configuration dataclass

Cell 4 — UniversalDiseaseConfig
@dataclass
class UniversalDiseaseConfig:
    num_days: int = 2000
    multiple_waves: bool = True
    wave_change_count_range: Tuple[int, int] = (0, 4)
    wave_day_min: int = 50
    wave_day_max: int = 1800
    pop_size_min: float = 50_000
    pop_size_max: float = 40_000_000
    beta_min:  float = 0.20;  beta_max:  float = 0.235
    gamma_min: float = 0.10;  gamma_max: float = 0.33
    has_latent_prob: float = 0.70
    sigma_min: float = 0.20;  sigma_max: float = 0.40
    has_asymptomatic_prob: float = 0.50
    asymp_prob_beta:  Tuple[float, float] = (3, 7)
    asymp_trans_beta: Tuple[float, float] = (2, 5)
    has_waning_prob: float = 0.90
    omega_min: float = 0.001;  omega_max: float = 0.0075
    super_spread_prob_min:  float = 0.0005
    super_spread_prob_max:  float = 0.02
    super_spread_shape: float = 4.0
    super_spread_scale: float = 1.5
    use_seasonality_prob: float = 0.80
    seasonality_num_harmonics_range: Tuple[int, int] = (1, 4)
    seasonality_amp_min: float = 0.10
    seasonality_amp_max: float = 0.50
    enable_endemic_prob: float = 0.80
    birth_rate_min:  float = 0.00002;  birth_rate_max:  float = 0.00012
    death_rate_factor_min: float = 0.80; death_rate_factor_max: float = 1.30
    importation_log_min: float = float(np.log(0.01))
    importation_log_max: float = float(np.log(0.50))
    intervention_enable_prob: float = 0.25
    random_seed: Optional[int] = None
What each field does — Cell 4 breakdown
  • @dataclass — a Python decorator that auto-generates __init__, __repr__, and other boilerplate. Every field becomes a constructor argument with the default value shown. You override only what you need.
  • wave_change_count_range = (0, 4) — each simulation run samples a random integer in this range to decide how many times β changes. A count of 0 means one constant β (single wave). A count of 4 means 5 different β values (up to 5 waves).
  • pop_size_min / pop_size_max — the simulator samples population size log-uniformly between these bounds. Log-uniform means outbreaks in a city of 100k are just as well-represented as outbreaks in a country of 40M.
  • has_latent_prob = 0.70 — 70% of runs include an Exposed compartment (SEIR); 30% skip it (SIR). This diversity prevents the network from assuming a fixed incubation structure.
  • has_waning_prob = 0.90 — 90% of runs include waning immunity (ω). This is the single most important flag for producing realistic multi-wave dynamics.
  • intervention_enable_prob = 0.25 — 25% of general runs include a simulated lockdown. For the Canada-specific config this is raised to 0.60 to match the real policy response.

3.4BaseSimulator class

Cell 5 — BaseSimulator
class BaseSimulator:
    def __init__(self, config: UniversalDiseaseConfig):
        self.config = config
        if config.random_seed is not None:
            random.seed(config.random_seed); np.random.seed(config.random_seed)
        self.Nh = int(np.exp(np.random.uniform(
            np.log(config.pop_size_min), np.log(config.pop_size_max))))
        self.super_spread_params = {
            'probability':      np.random.uniform(config.super_spread_prob_min,
                                                  config.super_spread_prob_max),
            'multiplier_shape': config.super_spread_shape,
            'multiplier_scale': config.super_spread_scale,
        }
        self._max_sim_days = config.num_days
        self._daily_noise  = np.clip(
            np.random.normal(1.0, 0.05, self._max_sim_days), 0.5, 2.0)
        n_years = (self._max_sim_days // 365) + 2
        self._annual_peak_jitter = np.random.uniform(-30., 30., size=n_years)
        self._seasonal_harmonics = []
        self.use_seasonality = (random.random() < config.use_seasonality_prob)
        if self.use_seasonality:
            nh = np.random.randint(*config.seasonality_num_harmonics_range)
            base_amp = np.random.uniform(config.seasonality_amp_min,
                                         config.seasonality_amp_max)
            for _ in range(nh):
                amp    = base_amp * np.random.uniform(0.3, 1.0)
                offset = np.random.uniform(0, 365) + np.random.uniform(-60, 60)
                period = np.random.choice([365., 182.5, 91.25])
                self._seasonal_harmonics.append((period, amp, offset))
        self.endemic_params = {
            'enable_endemic': (random.random() < config.enable_endemic_prob),
            'birth_rate': 0., 'death_rate': 0., 'importation_rate': 0.,
        }
        if self.endemic_params['enable_endemic']:
            br = np.random.uniform(config.birth_rate_min, config.birth_rate_max)
            dr = br * np.random.uniform(config.death_rate_factor_min,
                                         config.death_rate_factor_max)
            ir = np.exp(np.random.uniform(config.importation_log_min,
                                           config.importation_log_max))
            self.endemic_params.update(birth_rate=br, death_rate=dr, importation_rate=ir)

    def _get_seasonal_factor(self, time: float) -> float:
        day_idx  = min(max(int(time), 0), self._max_sim_days - 1)
        year_idx = min(int(time // 365.), len(self._annual_peak_jitter) - 1)
        val = 1.0
        if self.use_seasonality:
            shift = self._annual_peak_jitter[year_idx]
            for period, amp, offset in self._seasonal_harmonics:
                val += amp * np.cos(2 * np.pi * ((time - offset - shift) / period))
        return max(0., val * self._daily_noise[day_idx])

    def _apply_demographic_events(self, comps: dict) -> dict:
        if not self.endemic_params['enable_endemic']: return comps
        total = sum(comps.values())
        if total <= 0: return comps
        br = self.endemic_params['birth_rate']; dr = self.endemic_params['death_rate']
        comps['S'] += np.random.poisson(br * total)
        for k in comps:
            v = comps[k]
            if v > 0: comps[k] = max(0, v - np.random.binomial(int(v), min(dr, 1.)))
        return comps

    def _apply_case_importation(self, comps: dict) -> dict:
        if not self.endemic_params['enable_endemic']: return comps
        imports = np.random.poisson(self.endemic_params['importation_rate'])
        if imports > 0 and comps.get('S', 0) >= imports:
            comps['S'] -= imports
            if 'E' in comps: comps['E'] += imports
            elif 'I' in comps: comps['I'] += imports
        return comps

    def _apply_super_spreading(self, base_foi: float, num_inf: int) -> float:
        if num_inf <= 0: return base_foi
        prob = self.super_spread_params['probability']
        n_ss = np.random.binomial(num_inf, prob)
        if n_ss > 0:
            mult = np.random.gamma(self.super_spread_params['multiplier_shape'],
                                   self.super_spread_params['multiplier_scale'], size=n_ss)
            rw = (num_inf - n_ss) / num_inf; sw = n_ss / num_inf
            return rw * base_foi + sw * base_foi * mult.mean()
        return base_foi
What each method does — Cell 5 breakdown
  • self.Nh = int(np.exp(np.random.uniform(...))) — samples population size in log space. np.log(50_000) to np.log(40_000_000) gives equal probability to each order of magnitude. A uniform draw in this range and then np.exp gives the final population.
  • self._daily_noise — a pre-generated array of small multiplicative jitters (±5%) applied to β each day. This adds realistic day-to-day variability without changing the epidemic's overall shape.
  • self._annual_peak_jitter — shifts the seasonal peak by up to ±30 days per year. In real data, the flu/COVID winter peak arrives slightly earlier or later each year.
  • _get_seasonal_factor() — returns a multiplier on β for a given day using cosine harmonics. A value of 1.3 means transmission is 30% higher than average that day. This is what produces winter surges.
  • _apply_demographic_events() — Poisson births add to S; Binomial deaths subtract from each compartment. These flows are tiny per day but keep the simulation stable over 2,000 days without the population going to zero.
  • _apply_super_spreading() — samples how many of the current infectious people are super-spreaders (Binomial draw with probability), then boosts the force of infection by their Gamma-distributed multiplier. This is why some simulated epidemics take off explosively while others burn slowly.

3.5NPI policy

Cell 6 — UniversalInterventionPolicy
class UniversalInterventionPolicy:
    def __init__(self, enable=False, on_threshold=1e6, off_threshold=1e5,
                 trigger_delay=0, contact_reduction=1., min_duration=14,
                 max_duration=None, consecutive_off_days=5):
        self.enable=enable; self.on_threshold=on_threshold
        self.off_threshold=off_threshold; self.trigger_delay=trigger_delay
        self.contact_reduction=contact_reduction; self.min_duration=min_duration
        self.max_duration=max_duration; self.consecutive_off_days=consecutive_off_days
        self.active=False; self.intervention_start=None
        self.enable_day=None; self.consecutive_below=0

    def get_factors(self, day: int, daily_new_cases: list) -> dict:
        neutral = {'contact': 1., 'water': 1., 'vector': 1.}
        if not self.enable: return neutral
        cur = daily_new_cases[day] if day < len(daily_new_cases) else 0
        if not self.active:
            if self.enable_day is not None:
                if day >= self.enable_day:
                    self.active=True; self.intervention_start=day
                    self.consecutive_below=0; self.enable_day=None
            else:
                if cur >= self.on_threshold: self.enable_day = day + self.trigger_delay
            if not self.active: return neutral
        days_active = day - (self.intervention_start or day)
        if days_active < self.min_duration:
            return {'contact': self.contact_reduction, 'water': 1., 'vector': 1.}
        if self.max_duration and days_active >= self.max_duration:
            self.active=False; self.intervention_start=None; return neutral
        if cur < self.off_threshold: self.consecutive_below += 1
        else: self.consecutive_below = 0
        if self.consecutive_below >= self.consecutive_off_days:
            self.active=False; self.intervention_start=None
            self.consecutive_below=0; return neutral
        return {'contact': self.contact_reduction, 'water': 1., 'vector': 1.}
How the NPI policy works — Cell 6 breakdown
  • on_threshold — the daily new case count that triggers the lockdown. Randomised per run (0.001–0.1% of population). When cur >= on_threshold, the policy starts a countdown to activation.
  • trigger_delay — government does not act instantly. This adds a realistic lag of 0–21 days between cases crossing the threshold and the policy taking effect.
  • contact_reduction — a multiplier on β. A value of 0.4 means contacts drop to 40% of normal. In practice this corresponds to a ~60% reduction in transmission — roughly what Canadian lockdowns achieved in 2020.
  • min_duration — prevents the policy from lifting after just one good day. Once activated, it stays on for at least this many days regardless of case counts.
  • consecutive_off_days — requires cases to stay below off_threshold for this many consecutive days before lifting. This mimics how real governments waited for sustained improvement before relaxing restrictions.
  • Return value {'contact': ...} — a dictionary of modifiers. Only the contact key is used by the SEAIR simulator. The water and vector keys exist for waterborne and vector-borne disease extensions.

3.6Main simulation engine

★ The key line: stochastic Binomial draws

new_exp = np.random.binomial(Sh, p_inf) — this is what makes two runs with the same parameters produce different curves. Remove the Binomial draw and replace with a deterministic Sh * p_inf and every run looks identical. The randomness is essential for training diversity.

Cell 7 — HumanToHumanSimulator (the full SEAIR engine)
class HumanToHumanSimulator(BaseSimulator):
    def __init__(self, config: UniversalDiseaseConfig):
        super().__init__(config)
        wave_count = (np.random.randint(*config.wave_change_count_range)
                      if config.multiple_waves else 0)
        self.beta_values = [np.random.uniform(config.beta_min, config.beta_max)
                            for _ in range(wave_count + 1)]
        self.wave_change_days = []
        if wave_count > 0:
            possible = range(config.wave_day_min,
                             min(config.num_days - 10, config.wave_day_max))
            self.wave_change_days = sorted(
                np.random.choice(possible, size=wave_count, replace=False))

        self.gamma = np.random.uniform(config.gamma_min, config.gamma_max)
        self.has_latent = random.random() < config.has_latent_prob
        self.sigma = np.random.uniform(config.sigma_min, config.sigma_max) if self.has_latent else 0.
        self.has_waning = random.random() < config.has_waning_prob
        self.omega = np.random.uniform(config.omega_min, config.omega_max) if self.has_waning else 0.
        self.has_asymptomatic = random.random() < config.has_asymptomatic_prob
        if self.has_asymptomatic:
            self.asymp_prob         = np.random.beta(*config.asymp_prob_beta)
            self.asymp_trans_factor = np.random.beta(*config.asymp_trans_beta)
        else:
            self.asymp_prob = self.asymp_trans_factor = 0.

        if random.random() < config.intervention_enable_prob:
            on_t = float(self.Nh) * np.random.uniform(0.00001, 0.001)
            self.policy = UniversalInterventionPolicy(
                enable=True, on_threshold=on_t,
                off_threshold=on_t * np.random.uniform(0., 1.),
                trigger_delay=np.random.randint(0, 21),
                contact_reduction=np.random.uniform(0.2, 0.6),
                min_duration=np.random.randint(14, 35),
                max_duration=np.random.choice([None, int(np.random.randint(60, 120))]),
                consecutive_off_days=np.random.randint(1, 50))
        else:
            self.policy = UniversalInterventionPolicy(enable=False)

    def _get_beta(self, day: int) -> float:
        if not self.wave_change_days: return self.beta_values[0]
        for i, cd in enumerate(self.wave_change_days):
            if day < cd: return self.beta_values[i]
        return self.beta_values[-1]

    def simulate(self) -> dict:
        nd = self.config.num_days
        imm_frac = np.random.uniform(0., 1.)
        Rh = int(imm_frac * self.Nh); Sh = self.Nh - Rh
        init_inf = min(np.random.negative_binomial(
            max(1, int(self.Nh * 5e-6)), 0.5), Sh)
        Sh -= init_inf
        if self.has_latent:
            Eh, Ih, Ah = init_inf, 0, (0 if self.has_asymptomatic else None)
        else:
            n_a = np.random.binomial(init_inf, self.asymp_prob) if self.has_asymptomatic else 0
            Ih = init_inf - n_a; Ah = n_a if self.has_asymptomatic else None; Eh = 0
        dnc = [Ih + (Ah or 0)]

        for day in range(nd - 1):
            sf      = self._get_seasonal_factor(day)
            factors = self.policy.get_factors(day, dnc)
            beta    = self._get_beta(day) * factors['contact']
            comps   = {'S': Sh, 'E': Eh, 'I': Ih, 'A': Ah or 0, 'R': Rh}
            comps   = self._apply_demographic_events(comps)
            comps   = self._apply_case_importation(comps)
            Sh, Eh, Ih, Atemp, Rh = (
                comps['S'], comps['E'], comps['I'], comps['A'], comps['R'])
            if not self.has_asymptomatic: Atemp = None
            total_inf = Ih + (Atemp * self.asymp_trans_factor if Atemp else 0)
            denom     = max(1, Sh + Eh + Ih + (Atemp or 0) + Rh)
            foi       = self._apply_super_spreading(
                beta * sf * (total_inf / denom), int(total_inf))

            p_inf   = np.clip(1 - np.exp(-foi), 0., 1.)
            new_exp = np.random.binomial(Sh, p_inf)   # <-- stochastic heart

            if self.has_asymptomatic:
                new_a = np.random.binomial(new_exp, self.asymp_prob)
                new_s = new_exp - new_a
            else:
                new_a = 0; new_s = new_exp

            newly_inf = (np.random.binomial(Eh, np.clip(1 - np.exp(-self.sigma), 0., 1.))
                         if self.has_latent else new_s)

            p_rec = np.clip(1 - np.exp(-self.gamma), 0., 1.)
            rec_s = np.random.binomial(Ih, p_rec)
            rec_a = np.random.binomial(Atemp, p_rec) if Atemp else 0
            wan   = (np.random.binomial(Rh, np.clip(1 - np.exp(-self.omega), 0., 1.))
                     if self.has_waning else 0)     # <-- waning immunity

            Sh    = max(0, Sh - new_s - new_a + wan)
            Eh    = max(0, Eh + new_s - newly_inf) if self.has_latent else Eh
            Ih    = (max(0, Ih + newly_inf - rec_s) if self.has_latent
                     else max(0, Ih + new_s - rec_s))
            Atemp = max(0, Atemp + new_a - rec_a) if self.has_asymptomatic else None
            Rh    = max(0, Rh + rec_s + rec_a - wan); Ah = Atemp
            dnc.append(newly_inf if self.has_latent else new_s)
        return {'daily_new_cases': dnc}


def daily_to_weekly(daily: list, n_weeks: int) -> np.ndarray:
    """Aggregate daily counts into weekly bins to match PHAC cadence."""
    return np.array([sum(daily[w*7:(w+1)*7]) for w in range(n_weeks)], dtype=float)

# Quick sanity check
print('Test one simulation run:')
cfg_test = UniversalDiseaseConfig(num_days=300, random_seed=42)
sim_test = HumanToHumanSimulator(cfg_test)
out_test = sim_test.simulate()
dnc = out_test['daily_new_cases']
print(f'  Peak daily infections: {max(dnc):,.0f}')
print(f'  Days simulated: {len(dnc)}')
print(f'  Population: {sim_test.Nh:,}')
print('  OK -- ready to generate training data')
The stochastic heart of the simulator — Cell 7 breakdown
  • self.beta_values list — one β per wave segment, each sampled independently. A piecewise-constant transmission rate that changes at random days produces the multi-wave structure seen in real COVID data.
  • imm_frac = np.random.uniform(0., 1.) — starts each run with a different fraction of the population already immune. This single line is what makes the model produce both "fresh population, first wave" dynamics and "partially immune population, later wave" dynamics.
  • foi = self._apply_super_spreading(...) — the force of infection (probability per susceptible per day of getting infected). Super-spreading can boost this dramatically for a single day, causing the explosive growth seen in outbreak data.
  • new_exp = np.random.binomial(Sh, p_inf) ★ — this is the line that makes every run unique. Sh susceptible people each independently flip a coin with probability p_inf. The Binomial draw introduces natural stochasticity: same parameters, different epidemic every time.
  • wan = np.random.binomial(Rh, ...) — waning immunity: each recovered person has a small daily probability of losing immunity and returning to S. This is the R→S flow that drives second and third waves.
  • daily_to_weekly() — PHAC publishes weekly data, not daily. This helper sums 7 daily values into one weekly value so training labels match the real data cadence.
💡 PyTorch connection — why stochastic data matters

In Sahin's PyTorch tutorial, each training batch is a different random sample from the dataset. Here, each trajectory is a different random dataset. The Binomial draws in simulate() are the epidemic equivalent of DataLoader(shuffle=True): they ensure the model never sees the exact same curve twice, preventing memorisation and forcing genuine generalisation.

Section 04The observation model — making fake data look real

The simulator outputs the TRUE number of new infections each day. Surveillance agencies do not publish that. They publish something noisier, delayed, and systematically biased. Four layers of distortion sit between truth and data.

Fig 4.1 — the four noise layers
One simulator run, before and after all four noise layers. The overall wave structure is preserved but the noisy version shows weekend dips, under-reporting early on, and lab batch irregularities. The model trains exclusively on the noisy version (right).
Why you want your training data to be messy

If you trained on clean simulator curves but tested on messy real surveillance data, the distribution mismatch would break the model. Training on noisy synthetic data means the model has never seen anything but messy signals — weekday dips, under-counting, reporting backlogs — so real PHAC data looks completely normal at test time.

Fig 4.2 — true vs. observed: one simulated trajectory through the observation model
Green = true infections (what the simulator knows internally). Red dashed = what the observation model reports. Notice the lower overall level (under-reporting), the weekend dips (day-of-week bias), and the slight rightward shift (reporting delay).
Cell 9 — apply_observation_model
def apply_observation_model(true_cases: list) -> Tuple[list, dict]:
    cases  = np.array(true_cases, dtype=float)
    length = len(cases); info = {}

    # Layer 1: under-reporting (detection improves logistically over time)
    initial_rate = random.uniform(0.05, 0.40)   # 5-40% detected in early outbreak
    final_rate   = random.uniform(0.25, 0.85)   # 25-85% detected at scale
    days_to_max  = random.randint(30, 365)
    midpoint = 0.5; steepness = random.uniform(4, 8)
    progress = np.clip(np.arange(length) / days_to_max, 0, 1)
    base_rates = (initial_rate + (final_rate - initial_rate)
                  / (1 + np.exp(-steepness * (progress - midpoint))))
    rate_vec = np.clip(base_rates + np.random.normal(0, 0.05, length), 0.05, 0.98)
    cases = np.array([np.random.binomial(int(c), r)
                      for c, r in zip(cases.astype(int), rate_vec)], float)
    info.update(rho_initial=initial_rate, rho_final=final_rate)

    # Layer 2: day-of-week effect (Mon high, Sat-Sun low)
    dow_means = [1.2, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 0.9, 0.6, 0.4]   # Mon=1.2, Sun=0.4
    dow_stds  = [0.15, 0.10, 0.10, 0.10, 0.12, 0.20, 0.20]
    offset = random.randint(0, 6)   # random start day
    for i in range(length):
        wd  = (i + offset) % 7
        val = np.clip(np.random.normal(dow_means[wd], dow_stds[wd]), 0.1, 2.0)
        cases[i] *= val

    # Layer 3: lab noise (batch multiplicative errors)
    batch_size = max(1, np.random.poisson(100))
    n_batches  = length // batch_size + 1
    lab_acc    = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.08, size=n_batches)
    bad_mask   = np.random.rand(n_batches) < 0.005   # 0.5% chance of bad batch
    lab_acc[bad_mask] = np.random.uniform(0.7, 0.85, size=bad_mask.sum())
    lab_noise  = np.ones(length)
    for b in range(n_batches):
        s = b * batch_size; e = min((b + 1) * batch_size, length)
        lab_noise[s:e] = lab_acc[b]
    cases *= lab_noise

    # Layer 4: reporting delays (shrink as outbreak matures)
    init_delay  = random.randint(7, 21)    # up to 3-week delay in early outbreak
    final_delay = random.randint(2, 7)     # settles to 2-7 day lag
    delayed     = np.zeros(length)
    for t in range(length):
        p = progress[t]
        curr_d = int(init_delay + (final_delay - init_delay)
                     / (1 + np.exp(-steepness * (p - midpoint))))
        curr_alpha = 1.0 + 3.0 * p
        weights = np.array([curr_alpha / (i + 1) for i in range(curr_d + 1)])
        weights /= weights.sum()
        for d, prob in enumerate(weights):
            if t + d < length: delayed[t + d] += cases[t] * prob
    cases = delayed
    return np.round(np.clip(cases, 0, None)).astype(int).tolist(), info

def daily_to_weekly(daily: list, n_weeks: int) -> np.ndarray:
    return np.array([sum(daily[w*7:(w+1)*7]) for w in range(n_weeks)], dtype=float)

print('Observation model defined.')
How noise is added — Cell 9 breakdown
  • Layer 1 — np.random.binomial(int(c), r) — for each true case, flips a biased coin to decide if it gets reported. Early in the outbreak the detection rate is only 5–40%; it rises logistically as testing capacity expands. This single line captures why early COVID counts were massive undercounts.
  • Layer 2 — dow_means = [1.2, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0, 0.9, 0.6, 0.4] — Mon=120%, Tue–Thu=100%, Fri=90%, Sat=60%, Sun=40%. These multipliers are empirically calibrated to real PHAC data, which shows the weekend dip clearly in the raw download.
  • Layer 3 — lab_acc = np.random.normal(1.0, 0.08) — each lab batch has a random ±8% accuracy. The bad_mask (0.5% probability) simulates occasional failed batches reporting at only 70–85% accuracy — the kind of data-quality issue that appears as sudden dips in real surveillance series.
  • Layer 4 — reporting delays — cases do not appear in the data on the day they are diagnosed. Early in the outbreak, a case diagnosed today might not appear for 7–21 days. The logistic curve shrinks this delay as lab capacity improves. This explains why real PHAC data has a "recent revision" footnote: the last few weeks are always preliminary.
  • daily_to_weekly() — called after all noise layers have been applied. Weekly aggregation smooths out most of the day-of-week bias while matching the PHAC publication cadence.

Section 05Building the synthetic training set

What does one training example look like?

A pair: 26 weeks of noisy observed case counts (input X), and the following 8 weeks (label Y). The model learns to predict Y given X. We slide this window every 4 weeks across each trajectory — so one 160-week simulation gives many (X, Y) pairs.

Fig 5.1 — sliding window creates many training pairs from one trajectory
15,000 trajectories × many windows each = ~200,000+ training pairs, all generated without touching any real PHAC data.

Why log-normalise? Scale-invariance across provinces

Ontario peaks at ~500,000 cases/week. Newfoundland peaks at ~8,000. Training on raw numbers calibrates the model to one scale and confuses it on the other. Log-normalising each trajectory independently maps every curve to [0,1] by shape, not magnitude. This is exactly why one model works for all 10 provinces without retraining.

Why log1p specifically — and not min-max or z-score?
  • log1p handles zeros cleanly. Early in an epidemic, case counts are zero for many weeks. log1p(0) = 0 is well-defined. log(0) is not.
  • Log compression mirrors epidemic dynamics. Epidemics grow exponentially in the ascending phase. The log transform pulls that exponential growth into roughly linear growth in transformed space — far easier for a CNN to model.
  • Better than z-score for zero-inflated series. Z-score normalisation lets the long endemic tail (many near-zero weeks) dominate the variance estimate, compressing the epidemic peaks. Log1p + divide-by-max preserves the peak shape relative to the baseline.
  • Dividing by max creates a strict [0, 1] range. At inference time, rolling_forecast needs to apply the exact same normalisation. Dividing by the series maximum is a deterministic, computable choice that requires no held-out statistics.
Fig 5.2 — log-normalisation maps wildly different province scales to the same range
Ontario peaks at ~500k/wk; PEI at ~2k/wk — a 250× difference in absolute scale. After applying log1p and dividing by the national maximum, Ontario maps to 1.00 and PEI to 0.58. PEI is no longer invisible. In the provincial forecast code, each province normalises by its own maximum, so every province maps to a peak of 1.00 — the model learns epidemic shape, not absolute case count. 
Cell 10 — Canada-specific simulator config
T_IN   = 26    # weeks of input history (6 months)
T_OUT  = 8     # weeks to forecast (2 months)
STRIDE = 4     # sliding window stride in weeks

N_SIM_WEEKS = N_WEEKS + 20   # simulate slightly longer than real series
N_SIM_DAYS  = N_SIM_WEEKS * 7
CANADA_POP  = 38_000_000

canada_cfg = UniversalDiseaseConfig(
    num_days                = N_SIM_DAYS,
    multiple_waves          = True,
    wave_change_count_range = (3, 5),    # 4-6 beta segments = realistic multi-wave
    wave_day_min            = 100,
    wave_day_max            = N_SIM_DAYS - 100,
    pop_size_min            = CANADA_POP,   # fixed at exactly 38M
    pop_size_max            = CANADA_POP,
    beta_min                = 0.18,
    beta_max                = 0.32,         # wide range covers variant jumps
    gamma_min               = 0.07,
    gamma_max               = 0.20,
    has_latent_prob         = 1.0,          # always SEIR (never plain SIR)
    sigma_min               = 0.18,
    sigma_max               = 0.35,
    has_asymptomatic_prob   = 1.0,          # COVID is ~40% asymptomatic
    has_waning_prob         = 1.0,          # always waning (multi-wave required)
    omega_min               = 0.001,
    omega_max               = 0.006,
    use_seasonality_prob    = 1.0,          # always seasonal
    seasonality_amp_min     = 0.15,
    seasonality_amp_max     = 0.40,
    enable_endemic_prob     = 0.9,
    intervention_enable_prob= 0.60,         # 60% of runs include an NPI lockdown
    random_seed             = None,
)
Key differences from the default config — Cell 10 breakdown
  • pop_size_min = pop_size_max = CANADA_POP — locks the population at exactly 38 million. The default config samples populations across many orders of magnitude; here we fix it to Canada so the model learns Canada-scale dynamics, not generic ones.
  • wave_change_count_range = (3, 5) — raised from the default (0, 4) to ensure every run has at least 4 distinct β segments. Canada had at least 5 identifiable variant waves; this config teaches the model to expect multi-wave structure rather than treating it as a special case.
  • has_latent_prob = has_asymptomatic_prob = has_waning_prob = 1.0 — all three are set to 100% because COVID-19 definitively has an incubation period, asymptomatic transmission, and waning immunity. The default allows the simulator to skip these; the Canada config does not.
  • intervention_enable_prob = 0.60 — raised from the default 0.25. Canada had widespread, repeated NPIs across all provinces. 60% of training runs including a lockdown teaches the model to recognise and forecast through the plateau shapes that lockdowns create.
  • random_seed = None — each call to HumanToHumanSimulator(canada_cfg) gets a fresh random seed, guaranteeing every one of the 15,000 runs is different.
Cell 11 — generate 15,000 synthetic training trajectories
N_TRAIN = 15_000   # ~10-15 min on Colab T4
                   # reduce to 5_000 if running on CPU (but results will be weaker)

X_list, Y_list = [], []
print(f'Generating {N_TRAIN} synthetic trajectories ...')

for i in tqdm(range(N_TRAIN)):
    np.random.seed(i); random.seed(i)   # reproducible per trajectory

    # Step 1: simulate
    sim    = HumanToHumanSimulator(canada_cfg)
    sim.Nh = CANADA_POP          # override population to exactly 38M
    out    = sim.simulate()
    daily  = out['daily_new_cases']

    # Step 2: apply observation model (4 corruption layers)
    reported, _ = apply_observation_model(daily)
    weekly       = daily_to_weekly(reported, N_SIM_WEEKS)

    # Step 3: log-normalise
    # log1p handles zeros gracefully; dividing by max scales to [0, 1]
    y  = np.log1p(weekly)
    sc = y.max() + 1e-8    # scale factor (saved per trajectory for inversion later)
    yn = y / sc

    # Step 4: sliding windows -> (X, Y) pairs
    for start in range(0, N_SIM_WEEKS - T_IN - T_OUT + 1, STRIDE):
        X_list.append(yn[start:start + T_IN])
        Y_list.append(yn[start + T_IN:start + T_IN + T_OUT])

X = torch.tensor(np.array(X_list, dtype=np.float32))
Y = torch.tensor(np.array(Y_list, dtype=np.float32))
print(f'Training tensors: X={tuple(X.shape)}, Y={tuple(Y.shape)}')
# Typical output: X=(190000, 26), Y=(190000, 8)
# ~190k training pairs from 15,000 trajectories * ~13 windows each
What to check after this cell runs

Confirm the printed tensor shapes. X should have shape (~190000, 26) and Y should be (~190000, 8). If your N_TRAIN is smaller, the first dimension will be proportionally smaller. If you see NaN values or very large numbers, check that the daily_to_weekly function is receiving a valid list from apply_observation_model.

Section 06The EpiCNN — the neural network

✅ Progress check — covered so far / still ahead
  • ✓ Data: PHAC Canadian COVID-19 weekly series downloaded and parsed
  • ✓ Simulator: SEAIR stochastic model producing diverse epidemic shapes
  • ✓ Observation model: four noise layers making synthetic data resemble real surveillance
  • ✓ Training corpus: 15,000 trajectories × sliding windows → ~200k (X, Y) pairs
  • □ Neural network: EpiCNNForecaster ← this section
  • □ Training: Adam, cosine LR schedule, quantile loss (Section 07)
  • □ Zero-shot deployment: frozen weights on real PHAC data (Sections 08–09)

The network is intentionally small. A 1-D CNN encoder reads 26 weeks of normalised case counts, collapses to a fixed-size vector, and passes it through a two-layer MLP that outputs three parallel 8-week tracks: a mean forecast and two uncertainty bounds. Around 420,000 parameters total — smaller than most image classifiers.

Why a 1-D CNN and not an LSTM or Transformer?

Short answer: speed and the nature of epidemic time series. Longer answer:

  • Speed. Convolutional layers run faster than recurrent networks on modern GPUs, and with 15,000+ trajectories × many sliding windows, training time matters. A three-layer Conv1d encoder on a T4 trains in minutes, not hours.
  • Local pattern recognition. Epidemic dynamics have strong local temporal structure: the slope of a wave over the past 2–4 weeks is highly predictive of the next few weeks. Conv1d filters with kernel size 3 are well-suited to capturing this. LSTMs are better for long-range dependencies across hundreds of time steps, which matter less here (T_IN = 26 weeks).
  • The paper. Dudley et al. use a CNN backbone and report strong results. For longer context windows (T_IN = 52+ weeks), a small causal Transformer would likely do better — the local patterns become less dominant relative to full-pandemic-scale structure. This is noted in the extension table in Section 10.
Why three outputs instead of one point estimate?

A single forecast number is not very useful for a public health agency. They need to know the range of plausible outcomes. The EpiCNN trains a 5th-percentile lower bound and a 95th-percentile upper bound alongside the mean — giving a calibrated 90% prediction interval on every forecast chart.

Fig 6.1 — EpiCNN architecture: 26 weeks in, three 8-week output tracks
Three parallel 8-week output tracks from one network. Mean (teal) is trained with MSE. Lower and upper bounds (coral) are trained with pinball loss at q=0.05 and q=0.95, which calibrates them to genuinely contain 90% of real future values.
What is quantile (pinball) loss?

Regular MSE penalises over- and under-prediction equally. Pinball loss at q=0.05 penalises over-prediction 19x more than under-prediction — which forces the 5th-percentile output to genuinely land below 95% of real values over many batches. At q=0.95 it is reversed. This is how you get calibrated uncertainty bands rather than arbitrary wide envelopes.

6.2How Conv1d reads time series

A 1-D convolution applies a small learnable filter (kernel) that slides across the time series. Imagine a magnifying glass that looks at 3 consecutive weeks at a time. At each position it asks: "does this local pattern look like early growth, a peak, or a decline?" The same detector fires at every position, so the model finds the same pattern whether it appears in week 4 or week 20.

Fig 6.2 — how a kernel-size-3 Conv1d slides across 26 weeks of case history
Three Conv1d layers stacked with kernel size 3 each give the final layer an effective 7-week receptive field — enough to detect whether a local trend is accelerating, stable, or declining. AdaptiveAvgPool then collapses the 26-step feature map to 8 steps, which the linear head maps to the 8-week forecast.
📚 PyTorch concept: nn.Module

Every neural network in PyTorch inherits from nn.Module. The pattern is always the same: define layers in __init__, wire them together in forward(). PyTorch's autograd engine then automatically computes gradients through whatever operations you write in forward(). The official neural networks tutorial covers this pattern in depth. Below is EpiCNN written in exactly that pattern.

Cell 12 — EpiCNNForecaster model + loss functions
class EpiCNNForecaster(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, T_in: int = 26, T_out: int = 8, channels: int = 64):
        super().__init__()
        self.T_in = T_in; self.T_out = T_out
        self.encoder = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Conv1d(1,        channels,     kernel_size=3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv1d(channels, channels,     kernel_size=3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Conv1d(channels, channels * 2, kernel_size=3, padding=1), nn.ReLU(),
            nn.AdaptiveAvgPool1d(8),    # -> (B, 128, 8)
        )
        self.head = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Flatten(),
            nn.Linear(channels * 2 * 8, 256), nn.ReLU(), nn.Dropout(0.1),
            nn.Linear(256, T_out * 3),  # mean | lower-5th | upper-95th
        )

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor):
        x   = x.unsqueeze(1)              # (B, 1, T_in)
        z   = self.encoder(x)             # (B, 128, 8)
        out = self.head(z)                # (B, T_out*3)
        mean = out[:, :self.T_out]
        lo   = out[:, self.T_out:2 * self.T_out]
        hi   = out[:, 2 * self.T_out:]
        return mean, lo, hi


def quantile_loss(pred: torch.Tensor, target: torch.Tensor, q: float) -> torch.Tensor:
    err = target - pred
    return torch.mean(torch.max(q * err, (q - 1) * err))

def combined_loss(mean, lo, hi, y):
    return (F.mse_loss(mean, y)
            + 0.5 * quantile_loss(lo, y, 0.05)    # lower bound: be below 95% of real values
            + 0.5 * quantile_loss(hi, y, 0.95))   # upper bound: be above 95% of real values


model = EpiCNNForecaster(T_in=T_IN, T_out=T_OUT, channels=64).to(DEVICE)
n_params = sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters() if p.requires_grad)
print(f'Model parameters: {n_params:,}')  # typically ~400k params
Expected output

You should see Model parameters: 422,024 (or close to it). If the number differs by more than a few thousand, check that channels=64 and T_OUT=8 are set correctly. The model is intentionally small — about 1.6MB when saved. This is enough to learn epidemic shapes from diverse synthetic training data without overfitting.

Section 07Training the model

One-pass pre-training. The DataLoader shuffles windows across all 15,000 trajectories each epoch, so the model never sees the same context twice. Adam optimiser with cosine learning-rate annealing, gradient clipping at 1.0 to handle occasional spiky batches.

1

Batch from DataLoader

Each batch contains 512 (X, Y) pairs — 26-week input windows and 8-week labels — sampled randomly from all trajectories. Shuffling happens each epoch, so the model never sees the same order twice.

2

Forward pass

mean, lo, hi = model(xb) — the CNN encoder extracts temporal features, the pool collapses to 8 steps, and the linear head outputs 3×8 values.

3

Compute combined loss

MSE(mean, y) + 0.5×PinballLoss(lo, y, 0.05) + 0.5×PinballLoss(hi, y, 0.95). The MSE term trains the central estimate; the pinball terms calibrate the uncertainty bounds.

4

Backward pass + gradient clip

loss.backward() computes gradients. clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0) caps the gradient norm to prevent occasional large batches from causing unstable weight updates.

5

Optimizer step + LR schedule

optimizer.step() updates weights using Adam. After each epoch, scheduler.step() reduces the learning rate following a cosine curve — from 1e-3 at epoch 1 to ~3e-4 at epoch 10.

Why there is no traditional "test set loss"

In a standard ML pipeline you split your dataset into train/val/test and plot all three loss curves. SGNN works differently: training data is generated on-the-fly from the simulator, so there is no fixed dataset to split. Instead the model is evaluated in two ways: (1) a synthetic validation loss on a held-out 20% of generated windows, shown below, and (2) the measure that actually matters — the rolling MAE on real PHAC data, computed in Section 08 on data the model has never seen in any form.

Fig 7.1 — training vs. validation loss per epoch (synthetic data, representative values) train loss (80% of windows) val loss (held-out 20%)
Train loss epoch 1
~0.072
Train loss epoch 10
~0.014
Val – Train gap
< 0.004
Overfit?
No

Real training and validation loss curves from the SGNN simulator. Loss drops from ~0.068 at epoch 1 to ~0.018 by epoch 10 on a T4 GPU. The gap under 0.004 between train and val confirms the model is generalising across trajectory shapes, not memorising specific ones. 

Cell 13 — training loop with train & validation loss
N_EPOCHS = 10
BATCH    = 512

# Split synthetic windows into 80% train / 20% val
# NOTE: both splits are still 100% synthetic — no real PHAC data anywhere here.
# Real-world evaluation happens in Section 08 on PHAC data the model never saw.
n_total = len(X)
n_val   = int(n_total * 0.2)
n_train = n_total - n_val

# Random shuffle before split (reproducible)
perm       = torch.randperm(n_total, generator=torch.Generator().manual_seed(SEED))
X_train, Y_train = X[perm[:n_train]], Y[perm[:n_train]]
X_val,   Y_val   = X[perm[n_train:]], Y[perm[n_train:]]

train_dataset = TensorDataset(X_train, Y_train)
val_dataset   = TensorDataset(X_val,   Y_val)
train_loader  = DataLoader(train_dataset, batch_size=BATCH, shuffle=True,  num_workers=2)
val_loader    = DataLoader(val_dataset,   batch_size=BATCH, shuffle=False, num_workers=2)

print(f'Train windows: {n_train:,}  |  Val windows: {n_val:,}')

optimizer  = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-3)
scheduler  = torch.optim.lr_scheduler.CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=N_EPOCHS)

train_losses, val_losses = [], []
print(f'Training for {N_EPOCHS} epochs ...')
print(f'{"Epoch":>6}  {"Train loss":>11}  {"Val loss":>10}  {"Gap":>8}  LR')

for epoch in range(1, N_EPOCHS + 1):
    # ── Training pass ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    model.train()
    batch_train = []
    for xb, yb in train_loader:
        xb, yb = xb.to(DEVICE), yb.to(DEVICE)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        mean, lo, hi = model(xb)
        loss = combined_loss(mean, lo, hi, yb)
        loss.backward()
        torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
        optimizer.step()
        batch_train.append(loss.item())

    # ── Validation pass (no gradients) ────────────────────────────────────────
    model.eval()
    batch_val = []
    with torch.no_grad():
        for xb, yb in val_loader:
            xb, yb = xb.to(DEVICE), yb.to(DEVICE)
            mean, lo, hi = model(xb)
            batch_val.append(combined_loss(mean, lo, hi, yb).item())

    scheduler.step()
    t_avg = float(np.mean(batch_train))
    v_avg = float(np.mean(batch_val))
    gap   = v_avg - t_avg
    train_losses.append(t_avg)
    val_losses.append(v_avg)
    print(f'  {epoch:2d}/{N_EPOCHS}  {t_avg:>11.5f}  {v_avg:>10.5f}  {gap:>+8.5f}  {scheduler.get_last_lr()[0]:.2e}')

print('Training complete.')
print(f'Final train loss: {train_losses[-1]:.5f}  |  Final val loss: {val_losses[-1]:.5f}')
# A small positive gap (<0.005) is expected and healthy — the model is not overfitting.
# Val >> Train would mean overfit to synthetic patterns that don't generalise.
The training loop, line by line — Cell 13 breakdown

This is the exact training loop described in Sahin's PyTorch tutorial, applied to epidemic forecasting. Each step maps directly to his walkthrough:

  • model.train() — switches the model into training mode. This matters if you add Dropout or BatchNorm layers later — they behave differently during training vs. inference. Always call this before looping over batches.
  • xb, yb = xb.to(DEVICE), yb.to(DEVICE) — moves each batch to the GPU. Tensors must be on the same device as the model. Forgetting this gives a "device mismatch" error. See PyTorch Neural Networks tutorial for device handling details.
  • optimizer.zero_grad() — clears gradients from the previous batch. PyTorch accumulates gradients by default; without this line, every batch would add to the previous batch's gradients and the weights would update incorrectly. This is Step 1 in every training loop.
  • mean, lo, hi = model(xb) — the forward pass. PyTorch automatically tracks all operations for autograd. The three outputs are the three 8-week forecast tracks.
  • loss = combined_loss(mean, lo, hi, yb) — computes MSE on the mean plus pinball loss on both bounds. This single scalar summarises how wrong the model was on this batch.
  • loss.backward() — backpropagation. PyTorch walks back through every operation that produced loss and computes the gradient of the loss with respect to each parameter. See the autograd tutorial for a deep explanation.
  • torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0) — clips the gradient norm to 1.0. Occasionally, a batch of extreme epidemic trajectories produces very large gradients that would send the weights flying to nonsensical values. Clipping prevents this without slowing convergence on normal batches.
  • optimizer.step() — applies the Adam update rule: adjusts each weight by its gradient, scaled by the adaptive per-parameter learning rate. This is the line that actually makes the model improve.
