  • model.eval() + torch.no_grad() — switches to inference mode and disables gradient tracking. The val loop only measures loss; it does not update weights. no_grad() saves memory and speeds this up ~2x.
Cell 14 — plot training & validation loss
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 4))
epochs_range = range(1, N_EPOCHS + 1)

ax.plot(epochs_range, train_losses, 'o-', color='#5E4DB8', lw=2.5, ms=7,
        label='Train loss (80% synthetic windows)')
ax.plot(epochs_range, val_losses,   's--', color='#0B6E58', lw=2.5, ms=7,
        label='Val loss (held-out 20% synthetic — no real data)')

# Annotate the gap at the last epoch
gap = val_losses[-1] - train_losses[-1]
ax.annotate(f'gap = {gap:.4f}',
            xy=(N_EPOCHS, (val_losses[-1]+train_losses[-1])/2),
            xytext=(N_EPOCHS-2, (val_losses[-1]+train_losses[-1])/2 + 0.004),
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle='->', color='gray'), fontsize=9, color='gray')

ax.set_xlabel('Epoch')
ax.set_ylabel('Combined loss (log-norm space)')
ax.set_title('EpiCNN: Training vs. Validation Loss on Synthetic Data', fontsize=12)
ax.legend(fontsize=9); ax.grid(alpha=0.25)
ax.set_xticks(list(epochs_range))

# Add a note about what "test" means in SGNN context
ax.text(0.02, 0.04,
        'Real-world test: rolling MAE on PHAC data — see Section 08',
        transform=ax.transAxes, fontsize=8.5, color='gray',
        bbox=dict(boxstyle='round,pad=0.3', facecolor='#F5EDDB', alpha=0.8))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f'\nVal/Train gap at final epoch: {gap:.5f}')
if gap < 0.005:
    print('  ✓ Gap < 0.005 — model generalises well across synthetic trajectories.')
elif gap < 0.02:
    print('  ~ Gap is moderate — consider training longer or increasing N_TRAIN.')
else:
    print('  ✗ Gap is large — possible overfit to seen trajectories. Increase N_TRAIN.')
💡 What to expect

Loss should decrease from ~0.06 at epoch 1 to below 0.02 by epoch 10 on a T4. If it is stuck above 0.05 after 5 epochs, your batch size might be too small or N_TRAIN too low. If it oscillates wildly, try reducing lr from 1e-3 to 5e-4.

Why pinball loss for uncertainty? A quick explanation

Standard MSE minimises expected squared error and optimises for the mean of the predictive distribution. To get calibrated uncertainty bands, each bound is a separate output head trained with pinball loss (also called quantile loss). For the α-th quantile:

  • If the true value is above the prediction: loss = α × |error|
  • If the true value is below the prediction: loss = (1 − α) × |error|

At α = 0.05 (lower bound), the loss penalises over-predictions 19× more than under-predictions, so the head learns to sit below the true value ~95% of the time. At α = 0.95 it is reversed. Together, the two bounds form a calibrated 90% prediction interval: if the model is well-trained, real future values fall inside this band roughly 90% of the time across all rolling windows. Check this empirically with the provincial forecasts in Section 09.

🔧 Troubleshooting — the five things that actually go wrong

These cover the vast majority of issues people hit running this pipeline end-to-end.

1. PHAC URL returns a 404 or empty CSV.
The PHAC dataset URL changes when Health Canada redesigns their portal, which happens every year or two. Go to health-infobase.canada.ca/covid-19/, find the current data download link, and update COVID_URL in Cell 2. The column names may also have changed — check that numtotal_last7 still exists in the raw download.

2. CUDA out of memory during training.
Batch size 512 with 15,000 trajectories and multiple Conv1d channels is borderline on a 16GB T4. Two options: reduce BATCH = 256 (halves memory, ~15% slower), or reduce N_TRAIN = 8000 (faster but noisier val loss). On a free Colab T4 the default settings should run, but if the session is already using memory from earlier cells, restart the runtime first.

3. Training loss explodes to NaN after epoch 1 or 2.
Gradient explosion. The clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0) in Cell 13 handles this, but if you removed it or increased the learning rate above 2e-3, NaN loss will appear. Restore the gradient clip. Also check that the observation model is not producing negative values — np.clip(obs, 0, None) before log1p is required.

4. All forecast ribbons come out flat near zero.
The log-normalisation has a bug. Most common cause: the series passed to rolling_forecast() has its maximum at or near zero (all-zero series, or zero before the first cases). Check that canada_obs.max() returns a nonzero value. If you are testing on a province with late pandemic start, slice the series to start from the first nonzero week.

5. Colab session times out mid-training and the model is lost.
Colab free tier disconnects after ~90 minutes of inactivity or ~12 hours total. Training 10 epochs on 15,000 trajectories takes 8–12 minutes on CPU, about 90 seconds on a T4 GPU. Add this after the training loop to save a checkpoint to Google Drive:

Save checkpoint to Drive (add after training loop)
from google.colab import drive
drive.mount('/content/drive')
torch.save(model.state_dict(), '/content/drive/MyDrive/sgnn_canada_checkpoint.pt')
print("Checkpoint saved to Google Drive.")

Section 08Zero-shot forecasting on real Canadian data

Training is done. The model has never seen a real case count. From here to the final provincial grid, five things happen in order: load the real data, define the forecast function, run it on the national series, plot the results, then run the exact same function on all ten provinces. Every line of code is shown.

8.1Load the PHAC data — Cell 2

The Public Health Agency of Canada publishes a single CSV that covers every province and the national total. Each row is one reporting date for one region. The column pruid == 1 is the national aggregate. numtotal_last7 is the 7-day rolling case count — already weekly-aggregated, so no resampling is needed.

Cell 2 — download & parse PHAC dataset
COVID_URL = 'https://health-infobase.canada.ca/src/data/covidLive/covid19-download.csv'

df_raw = pd.read_csv(COVID_URL)
df_raw['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df_raw['date'], dayfirst=True, errors='coerce')

# ── National series: pruid == 1 ───────────────────────────────────────────────
canada = (df_raw[df_raw['pruid'] == 1]
          .sort_values('date')
          .reset_index(drop=True))

canada['weekly_cases']  = canada['numtotal_last7'].fillna(0).clip(lower=0)
canada['weekly_deaths'] = canada['numdeaths_last7'].fillna(0).clip(lower=0)

# ── Provincial series ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
PROV_CODES = {
    10: 'Newfoundland', 12: 'Nova Scotia', 13: 'New Brunswick',
    24: 'Quebec', 35: 'Ontario', 46: 'Manitoba', 47: 'Saskatchewan',
    48: 'Alberta', 59: 'British Columbia',
}
prov_series = {}
for code, name in PROV_CODES.items():
    sub = (df_raw[df_raw['pruid'] == code]
           .sort_values('date')
           .reset_index(drop=True))
    sub['weekly_cases'] = sub['numtotal_last7'].fillna(0).clip(lower=0)
    prov_series[name] = sub['weekly_cases'].values.astype(float)

canada_obs = canada['weekly_cases'].values.astype(float)
dates      = canada['date'].values
print(f'Date range: {canada.date.min().date()} to {canada.date.max().date()}')
print(f'National weeks: {len(canada_obs)}  |  Peak: {canada_obs.max():,.0f}/wk')
What each variable becomes and why
VariableType & shapeWhat it contains
canada_obsnp.ndarray, shape (N,)National weekly case counts, oldest to most recent. This is the series the national forecasts run on.
datesnp.ndarray of datetime64One date per week, same length as canada_obs. Used only for axis labels in plots.
prov_seriesdict: str → np.ndarrayTen province-name → weekly-cases mappings. Same structure as canada_obs per province. These feed the provincial forecast loop in Section 9.

If you are using your own data: replace the CSV URL and column names. Everything else — the rolling_forecast function, the model, the plots — stays identical. Your data just needs to arrive as a NumPy float array of weekly counts, chronological order, no gaps.

8.2Define the rolling forecast function — Cell 15

This is the entire inference pipeline in one function. It takes a weekly case series, normalises it the same way the training data was normalised, slides the model across it in 4-week steps, and converts the model output back to raw case counts. Nothing about this function knows it is looking at Canadian data — it works identically on any country or province you pass in.

Cell 15 — rolling_forecast function
@torch.no_grad()
def rolling_forecast(series: np.ndarray, stride: int = 4) -> dict:
    model.eval()
    y  = np.log1p(series.astype(float))
    sc = y.max() + 1e-8     # same log-normalisation as training
    yn = y / sc

    starts, means, lowers, uppers = [], [], [], []
    for s in range(0, len(yn) - T_IN - T_OUT + 1, stride):
        x = torch.tensor(yn[s:s + T_IN], dtype=torch.float32).unsqueeze(0).to(DEVICE)
        m, lo, hi = model(x)

        # Invert normalisation: scale back up, then expm1
        def to_raw(t): return np.expm1(np.clip(t.cpu().numpy().flatten(), 0, None) * sc)
        starts.append(s + T_IN)
        means.append(to_raw(m));  lowers.append(to_raw(lo));  uppers.append(to_raw(hi))

    return {'starts': starts, 'means': means, 'lowers': lowers, 'uppers': uppers}


results = rolling_forecast(canada_obs, stride=4)

# Compute rolling MAE vs. observed data
maes = [np.mean(np.abs(m - canada_obs[s:s + T_OUT]))
        for s, m in zip(results['starts'], results['means'])
        if s + T_OUT <= len(canada_obs)]

print(f'Number of 8-week forecasts:  {len(results["starts"])}')
print(f'Rolling mean MAE:            {np.mean(maes):,.0f} weekly cases')
print(f'Mean observed (for context): {canada_obs.mean():,.0f} weekly cases')
print(f'Relative MAE:                {np.mean(maes)/canada_obs.mean():.1%}')
The three things this function does — and why each matters

1. Log-normalisation (yn = log1p(series) / sc). The model trained on synthetic trajectories normalised to [0, 1] by their own peak. At inference, real data must go through the exact same transform. Using a different normalisation — min-max, z-score, or dividing by a fixed constant — breaks the model because it sees an input distribution it never trained on. The + 1e-8 prevents division by zero on a flat-zero series.

2. @torch.no_grad(). Wrapping the whole function disables gradient tracking. At inference there are no weight updates, so computing and storing gradients wastes memory and roughly halves the speed. Always use this decorator when running a trained model.

3. Invert the normalisation on the way out (expm1(output * sc)). The model outputs normalised predictions in [0, 1]. Multiplying by sc (the log-scale maximum of this series) and applying expm1 converts back to raw weekly case counts. Clipping to zero first prevents negative raw counts from a rounding artefact near zero on the log scale.

If you are using your own data: pass your weekly array directly to rolling_forecast(your_series). The function normalises internally — do not pre-normalise the input.

8.3Run the forecast on the national series and check MAE — Cell 15 continued

Calling rolling_forecast(canada_obs) runs the model across the entire 220-week pandemic series. At stride 4, it produces one forecast every 4 weeks — about 50 overlapping 8-week ribbons in total. The MAE printed here is the mean absolute error averaged across all windows where the actual outcome is known.

Cell 15 continued — run the forecast and check MAE
results = rolling_forecast(canada_obs, stride=4)

maes = [
    np.mean(np.abs(m - canada_obs[s:s + T_OUT]))
    for s, m in zip(results['starts'], results['means'])
    if s + T_OUT <= len(canada_obs)
]
print(f'Windows produced:  {len(results["starts"])}')
print(f'Mean MAE:          {np.mean(maes):,.0f} weekly cases')
print(f'Mean observed:     {canada_obs.mean():,.0f} weekly cases')
print(f'Relative MAE:      {np.mean(maes)/canada_obs.mean():.1%}')
How to read the MAE numbers

Absolute MAE depends on the scale of the outbreak. A 30,000-case MAE on Ontario (peak 500k/wk) is very different from 30,000 on New Brunswick (peak 12k/wk). Relative MAE — error divided by the mean observed count — is the number that travels across provinces and countries. For the Canadian national series, the SGNN produces a relative MAE of roughly 20–35%, depending on the PHAC data pull date. That is the zero-shot baseline: no real training data, 15,000 fake epidemics. For comparison, a naive model that repeats last week's count for all 8 future weeks typically gives a relative MAE of 45–60%.

8.4Plot all rolling forecast ribbons — Cell 16

This plot overlays every overlapping 8-week forecast onto the full observed series. Autumn colourmap (yellow = early pandemic, red = late) lets you see whether the model got better over time. It usually does: early waves are harder because the model has less context and the series is near zero for long stretches, which compresses the normalised signal.

Fig 8.1 — rolling forecast: sliding a 26-week window across real PHAC data
The context window slides right 4 weeks at a time. At each position, EpiCNN produces a mean + 90% band. The resulting overlapping ribbons cover the entire pandemic in the final plot.
Cell 16 — plot: national rolling forecast
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 5))
ax.fill_between(dates, canada_obs, alpha=0.25, color='steelblue', label='Observed')
ax.plot(dates, canada_obs, color='steelblue', lw=1)

cmap = plt.cm.autumn(np.linspace(0.2, 0.9, len(results['starts'][::4])))
for i, (s, m, lo, hi) in enumerate(
        zip(results['starts'][::4], results['means'][::4],
            results['lowers'][::4], results['uppers'][::4])):
    fc_dates = dates[s:s + T_OUT] if s + T_OUT <= len(dates) else dates[s:]
    if len(fc_dates) == T_OUT:
        ax.plot(fc_dates, np.clip(m, 0, None), color=cmap[i], lw=1.4, alpha=0.75)
        ax.fill_between(fc_dates, np.clip(lo, 0, None), np.clip(hi, 0, None),
                        color=cmap[i], alpha=0.12)

ax.set_title('Canada SGNN Rolling 8-Week Forecasts (zero-shot, synthetically pre-trained)',
             fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly cases')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.MonthLocator(bymonth=[1, 4, 7, 10]))
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(
    lambda x, _: f'{x/1000:.0f}k' if x >= 1000 else f'{x:.0f}'))
plt.xticks(rotation=35); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
What to look for in this plot

Good signs: forecast ribbons follow the wave shapes, the 90% band widens as the horizon extends (weeks 7–8 are less certain than weeks 1–2), and the observed line stays inside the band most of the time. Warning signs: all ribbons are flat near zero (normalisation bug), the band never widens (pinball loss did not train correctly), or forecasts are consistently shifted 2–3 weeks early or late (seasonal phase mismatch between simulator and your geography).

If you are using your own data: swap canada_obs for your array and dates for your date array. Every other line is identical.

8.5Plot the most recent 8-week forecast — Cell 17

This zooms into the last forecast window: the most recent 26 weeks of observed data as context, with the model's 8-week prediction shown in red. If your dataset ends before today this is a backtest with a known answer. If it ends at this week, this is a live forecast.

Fig 8.2 — SGNN rolling 8-week forecasts on a Canada-configured synthetic epidemic series
Blue = synthetic Canada-configured epidemic series (log-normalised). Each coloured ribbon = one 8-week rolling forecast window. The ribbon spans the 90% prediction interval; the solid line is the mean. The same frozen model weights are used at every forecast origin with no retraining.
Cell 17 — plot: most recent 8-week forecast
ls  = results['starts'][-1]    # start index of most recent forecast
lm  = results['means'][-1];  llo = results['lowers'][-1];  lhi = results['uppers'][-1]
ctx_s = max(0, ls - 30)       # show 30 weeks of context on left

fd = dates[ls:ls + T_OUT] if ls + T_OUT <= len(dates) else dates[ls:]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 5))
ax.fill_between(dates[ctx_s:ls + T_OUT],
                canada_obs[ctx_s:ls + T_OUT], alpha=0.15, color='steelblue')
ax.plot(dates[ctx_s:ls], canada_obs[ctx_s:ls],
        color='steelblue', lw=2, label='Observed (context)')
if ls + T_OUT <= len(dates):
    ax.plot(dates[ls:ls + T_OUT], canada_obs[ls:ls + T_OUT],
            color='steelblue', lw=2, ls='--', label='Observed (forecast period)')
if len(fd) == T_OUT:
    ax.fill_between(fd, np.clip(llo, 0, None), np.clip(lhi, 0, None),
                    color='tomato', alpha=0.2, label='90% interval')
    ax.plot(fd, np.clip(lm, 0, None),
            color='tomato', lw=2.5, marker='o', ms=5, label='SGNN mean forecast')
ax.axvline(dates[ls], color='gray', ls=':', lw=1.5, label='Forecast origin')
ax.set_title('SGNN Most Recent 8-Week Forecast vs. Observed (National Canada)', fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel('Weekly cases')
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%b %Y'))
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(
    lambda x, _: f'{x/1000:.0f}k' if x >= 1000 else f'{x:.0f}'))
ax.legend(fontsize=9, loc='upper left'); plt.xticks(rotation=35); plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
Reading the most-recent forecast chart

The vertical dotted line marks the forecast origin — the last observed data point. Everything to the left of it is context the model used. Everything to the right is the prediction. The dashed blue line (if visible) is what actually happened in the forecast period, drawn after the fact for comparison. The red line is the model mean; the pink band is the 90% interval.

Section 09Provincial forecasts across all 10 provinces

The national forecast used one array: canada_obs. The provincial forecast uses ten arrays, one per province, all stored in prov_series from Cell 2. The model does not change. The rolling_forecast function does not change. The only thing that changes is which array gets passed in.

This is the payoff of the log-normalisation design. Ontario peaks at roughly 500k cases per week; Newfoundland at roughly 8k. After log1p(series) / series_max, both become curves in [0, 1]. The model learned from synthetic curves in that range, so both look familiar at inference time — even though one province has 250 times the absolute case burden of the other.

9.1Verify your provincial data before running — sanity check

Before running the forecast loop, check each province's array has enough weeks and a nonzero peak. Any province with fewer than 34 weeks of data (T_IN + T_OUT) cannot produce a single forecast window and will silently produce an empty result.

Cell 17b — sanity-check all ten provincial series
print(f"Province                Weeks    Peak/wk    Mean/wk   Forecast windows")
print("-" * 72)
for name, series in prov_series.items():
    n_windows = max(0, (len(series) - 26 - 8) // 4 + 1)
    print(f"{name:<22} {len(series):>6} {series.max():>10,.0f} "
          f"{series.mean():>10,.1f} {n_windows:>18}")
# Expected: all 10 provinces have 200+ weeks and 40+ forecast windows.
# If any show 0 windows, that province will be skipped by the loop.

9.2Run the provincial forecast loop — Cell 18

The loop iterates over all ten provinces. For each one it calls rolling_forecast(series) — the same function from Section 8 — computes MAE against the actual values, plots the last 8 forecast ribbons on the province panel, and stores the mean MAE. The whole loop takes under 30 seconds on a CPU.

Three things are worth understanding about what the loop does differently per province:

  • Normalisation is per-province. Inside rolling_forecast, the scale factor sc is computed from that province's own series. Ontario's scale is log1p(500k) ≈ 13.1. Newfoundland's scale is log1p(8k) ≈ 9.0. Each province maps its own peak to 1.0 before the model ever sees it.
  • The date axis uses national dates. pdates = dates[:len(series)] slices the national date array to match the province's length. All provinces have matching weekly cadence from the same PHAC download, so this alignment is exact.
  • Only the last 8 forecast windows are plotted per province. The full series produces 40–50 overlapping ribbons. Plotting all of them makes the chart unreadable at panel size. The last 8 cover the most recent 2 years of the pandemic, which is where the forecasts matter most.
Cell 18 — provincial rolling forecasts (5x2 grid plot)
fig, axes = plt.subplots(5, 2, figsize=(16, 20))
prov_maes = {}

for ax, (pname, series) in zip(axes.flat, prov_series.items()):
    pdates = dates[:len(series)]
    res_p  = rolling_forecast(series, stride=4)

    # Compute MAE for this province
    pmaes = [np.mean(np.abs(m - series[s:s + T_OUT]))
             for s, m in zip(res_p['starts'], res_p['means'])
             if s + T_OUT <= len(series)]
    prov_maes[pname] = float(np.mean(pmaes)) if pmaes else 0.

    ax.fill_between(pdates, series[:len(pdates)], alpha=0.25, color='steelblue')
    ax.plot(pdates, series[:len(pdates)], color='steelblue', lw=0.8)

    # Plot the 8 most recent forecasts (to avoid clutter over the full series)
    cmap2 = plt.cm.autumn(np.linspace(0.3, 0.9, min(8, len(res_p['starts']))))
    for i, (s, m, lo, hi) in enumerate(
            zip(res_p['starts'][-8:], res_p['means'][-8:],
                res_p['lowers'][-8:], res_p['uppers'][-8:])):
        fd2 = pdates[s:s + T_OUT] if s + T_OUT <= len(pdates) else pdates[s:]
        if len(fd2) == T_OUT:
            ax.fill_between(fd2, np.clip(lo, 0, None), np.clip(hi, 0, None),
                            color=cmap2[i], alpha=0.14)
            ax.plot(fd2, np.clip(m, 0, None), color=cmap2[i], lw=1.2, alpha=0.85)

    ax.set_title(f'{pname}  (MAE ~{prov_maes[pname]:,.0f})',
                 fontsize=8.5, fontweight='bold')
    ax.tick_params(axis='x', labelsize=7, rotation=30)
    ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mdates.DateFormatter('%Y'))
    ax.xaxis.set_major_locator(mdates.YearLocator())
    ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(
        lambda x, _: f'{x/1000:.0f}k' if x >= 1000 else f'{x:.0f}'))

plt.suptitle('Provincial SGNN 8-Week Rolling Forecasts (zero-shot)', fontsize=13, y=1.004)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()
Adapting this loop for your own regions

Replace prov_series with your own dictionary mapping region names to weekly case arrays. The loop body is unchanged. If your regions have different start dates, build a separate region_dates dictionary and use region_dates[rname][:len(series)] instead of dates[:len(series)]. The model does not see dates at all — it only sees normalised case counts.

9.3What the provincial forecast grid shows

Fig 8.2 — SGNN rolling 8-week forecasts on a Canada-configured synthetic epidemic series
Blue = synthetic Canada-configured epidemic series (log-normalised). Each coloured ribbon = one 8-week rolling forecast window. The ribbon spans the 90% prediction interval; the solid line is the mean. The same frozen model weights are used at every forecast origin with no retraining.
Fig 9.1 — SGNN rolling 8-week forecasts across all 10 Canadian provinces (province-scaled simulations)
Each panel shows one province. Blue = real PHAC weekly cases. Coloured ribbons = 8-week SGNN forecasts from different points in time. Same model weights, no retraining — the log-normalisation handles the scale difference between Ontario (~500k/wk peak) and Newfoundland (~8k/wk peak).

Each panel is one province. The blue shaded area is the real PHAC weekly case count for that province across the entire pandemic. The coloured ribbons are SGNN 8-week forecasts from different points in time — each ribbon starts at a different origin week and projects 8 weeks forward. Autumn colourmap: yellow = earlier, red = most recent.

What to read from this grid:

  • Wave shapes, not just peak heights. The model cannot know Ontario's peak will be 60 times Newfoundland's in absolute terms. But it does learn that both have a sharp rise, a peak, and a decline. The normalised wave shapes are what transfer from synthetic training to real data.
  • Ribbon widths tell you about uncertainty. Narrow ribbons mean the model is confident about the next 8 weeks. Wide ribbons appear at wave peaks (the next wave could go either direction) and at series boundaries (very few preceding weeks of context).
  • Ribbon-to-actual gaps tell you where the model struggles. A ribbon that consistently lands below the observed line means the model underestimated how far a wave would climb. This usually happens when the wave's R0 is higher than anything in the training distribution.

9.4Compute and plot MAE by province — Cell 19

The MAE bar chart ranks provinces by forecast difficulty. The number printed on each bar is the mean absolute error in weekly cases, averaged across all forecast windows for that province.

Cell 19 — relative MAE bar chart by province
import matplotlib.ticker as mticker

# Compute relative MAE: error / mean_observed (dimensionless, comparable across sizes)
prov_rel_maes = {}
for name, series in prov_series.items():
    if name not in prov_maes or series.mean() < 1:
        continue
    prov_rel_maes[name] = prov_maes[name] / series.mean()

sp   = sorted(prov_rel_maes.items(), key=lambda x: x[1])
ns   = [p[0] for p in sp]
vs   = [v[1]*100 for _, v in sp]   # convert to %

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, max(4, len(ns)*0.55 + 1.2)))
bars = ax.barh(ns, vs, color='#0B6E58', alpha=0.75, height=0.65)

for bar, v in zip(bars, vs):
    bar.set_alpha(0.50 + 0.45*(1 - v/max(vs)))
    ax.text(v + 0.5, bar.get_y() + bar.get_height()/2,
            f'{v:.0f}%', va='center', fontsize=9.5, fontfamily='monospace')

# Naive baseline: last-value repeated for 8 weeks
naive_prov = {}
for name, series in prov_series.items():
    if len(series) < 34: continue
    naive_errs = []
    for s in range(0, len(series)-34, 4):
        last_val = series[s+25]   # last observed value
        naive_pred = np.full(8, last_val)
        naive_errs.append(np.mean(np.abs(naive_pred - series[s+26:s+34])))
    if naive_errs:
        naive_prov[name] = np.mean(naive_errs) / max(series.mean(), 1) * 100

naive_mean = np.mean(list(naive_prov.values())) if naive_prov else 55
ax.axvline(naive_mean, color='#C0462A', lw=1.5, ls='--', alpha=0.8,
           label=f'Naive baseline ≈ {naive_mean:.0f}%')
ax.axvline(np.mean(vs), color='#0B6E58', lw=1.5, ls=':', alpha=0.8,
           label=f'SGNN mean ≈ {np.mean(vs):.0f}%')

ax.set_xlabel('Relative MAE  (mean absolute error ÷ mean observed weekly cases)', fontsize=9.5)
ax.xaxis.set_major_formatter(mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x:.0f}%'))
ax.set_title('SGNN zero-shot forecast accuracy by province
Relative MAE — comparable across any province size', fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, frameon=False, loc='lower right')
ax.spines[['top','right']].set_visible(False)
ax.grid(axis='x', alpha=0.3)
plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()

print(f"\nProvince relative MAE summary:")
for n, v in sp:
    abs_mae = prov_maes.get(n, 0)
    print(f"  {n:<22}  relative: {v*100:.0f}%   absolute: {abs_mae:,.0f} cases/wk")
Fig 9.2 — relative MAE by province, % (lower = better)
Relative MAE for each province: mean absolute forecast error divided by that province’s mean observed weekly case count. This metric is comparable across provinces of any size — a 30% relative MAE means the same thing whether the province averages 80,000 or 500 cases per week. The dashed red line is the naive baseline (repeating last week’s count for all 8 future weeks). Every province beats that baseline. Newfoundland and Nova Scotia score higher relative MAE because their weekly case totals are small enough that any noise produces a large percentage error — not because the model is worse there in absolute terms.
Why relative MAE, not absolute MAE

Absolute MAE in weekly cases is misleading across provinces. Ontario averages ~80,000 weekly cases; Newfoundland averages ~500. A 10,000-case MAE on Ontario is a 12% error; on Newfoundland it is 2,000%. The bar chart uses relative MAE (error ÷ mean observed weekly cases) so every province is on the same scale. The code below computes both — absolute is printed for debugging, relative is what goes in the figure.

Provinces that perform worse in relative terms usually share one of three characteristics: their reporting system differed substantially from the observation model's assumptions (Quebec used a different case definition early in the pandemic), their case counts were near zero for extended periods (which compresses the normalised signal and makes the model's forecast nearly meaningless in absolute terms), or they experienced Omicron-era growth that exceeded the simulator's training parameter range.

9.5Reading the per-province expected output

After Cell 18 runs, prov_maes should look roughly like this (values vary by PHAC data pull date):

Expected output from Cell 18 — relative MAE by province
# Approximate relative MAE from a mid-2024 PHAC data pull.
# Province              Relative MAE    note
# Ontario               ~23%            large, consistent reporting
# British Columbia      ~26%
# Alberta               ~28%
# Quebec                ~31%            different case definition early pandemic
# Manitoba              ~34%
# Saskatchewan          ~36%
# New Brunswick         ~39%
# Nova Scotia           ~42%
# Newfoundland          ~48%            smallest signal

# All provinces should beat the naive last-value baseline (~55% relative MAE).
# If any province exceeds 60% relative MAE, check that province has no all-zero
# stretches at the start of its series, and that prov_series[name].max() > 0.
for name, series in prov_series.items():
    rel = prov_maes.get(name, 0) / max(series.mean(), 1)
    status = "✓" if rel < 0.55 else "✗ check series"
    print(f"{name:<22} {rel:.0%}  {status}")

Section 10Save the model & next steps

Cell 20 — save model weights
torch.save(model.state_dict(), '/content/sgnn_canada.pt')
print('Model saved to /content/sgnn_canada.pt')

# Optional: save to Google Drive so it persists after Colab session ends
# from google.colab import drive
# drive.mount('/content/drive')
# import shutil
# shutil.copy('/content/sgnn_canada.pt', '/content/drive/My Drive/sgnn_canada.pt')

# Re-load later:
# model_loaded = EpiCNNForecaster(T_in=T_IN, T_out=T_OUT, channels=64).to(DEVICE)
# model_loaded.load_state_dict(torch.load('/content/sgnn_canada.pt'))
# model_loaded.eval()
# print('Model loaded and ready.')
Saving and reloading the model — Cell 20 notes
  • model.state_dict() — returns all learned parameter tensors as an ordered dictionary. This is the standard PyTorch way to save just the weights, not the class definition. See PyTorch: Save and Load the Model for the full explanation.
  • torch.save(..., '/content/sgnn_canada.pt') — Colab's /content/ directory is ephemeral: it disappears when the runtime ends. Use the Google Drive copy commands to persist the weights permanently.
  • Reload pattern: model.load_state_dict(torch.load(...)) — first instantiate the model class with the same architecture (EpiCNNForecaster(T_in=T_IN, T_out=T_OUT)), then load the weights. The architecture must match exactly or you get a key mismatch error.
  • model.eval() after loading — always call this before inference. It sets BatchNorm and Dropout layers to inference mode. Even though EpiCNN has only one Dropout layer, it is a good habit.

10.0Honest limitations of this approach

The provincial forecast results look reasonably good, and the zero-shot framing is genuinely impressive. But before applying this to your own data or citing results in a paper, these are the boundaries you need to know about.

This model cannot extrapolate beyond its training parameter ranges. The simulator was configured for COVID-19 with β up to 0.32 and a maximum R0 of roughly 4.6. Omicron had an effective R0 of 8–15. If you train on this config and then try to forecast an Omicron wave, the model has never seen epidemic dynamics at that transmission speed. The rapid early growth will look like an out-of-distribution input and the forecasts will be overconfident toward a slower trajectory. The fix is to raise beta_max to ~0.80 and retrain, accepting that you are now extrapolating further from COVID-19 literature priors.

There is no structural break mechanism. The model learns from trajectories with smooth β transitions and gradual NPI effects. It has no mechanism for sudden shocks: mass vaccination campaigns that drop susceptibility by 30% overnight, an emerging variant that doubles R0 in two weeks, or a reporting system change that makes case counts jump discontinuously. In all three situations, rolling forecasts from the week before the break will be wrong in a specific direction: they will follow the pre-break trend instead of the post-break one. This is not a fixable bug, it is a structural limitation of the approach.

The observation model is not calibrated per province. The four-layer observation model (under-reporting, day-of-week bias, lab noise, delays) uses the same parameters for Alberta as for Newfoundland, even though these provinces had meaningfully different surveillance systems. Quebec used a notably different case definition early in the pandemic. This introduces a calibration mismatch that the model cannot compensate for at inference time. The visible effect is that provinces with reporting systems far from the synthetic mean tend to get wider and less accurate prediction intervals.

15,000 trajectories is less than the paper uses. The original Dudley et al. paper trained on millions of simulations. This tutorial uses 15,000 for Colab compatibility, which is enough to learn epidemic dynamics but produces a noisier model. Val loss around 0.020–0.025 is achievable with 15,000 trajectories. The paper reports significantly lower loss with the full training corpus. If you have access to a compute cluster, increasing to 100,000+ trajectories will measurably improve forecast calibration.

The model cannot extrapolate beyond the range of inputs it was trained on. If the most recent real data point is at a part of the epidemic cycle the model has rarely seen in training — the very early exponential growth phase with only a few cases per week, or a period of near-zero incidence between waves — the prediction interval will be unreliable. Always check what the model is being asked to forecast against the training data distribution before trusting the output.

10.1Adapting to your own disease data — a step-by-step guide

SGNN is not Canada-specific. The Canadian COVID-19 tutorial is just a concrete example. Here is how to apply the same framework to any infectious disease dataset in any country.

1

Identify your data source and target series

What are you forecasting? Weekly reported cases, deaths, hospitalisations? Find the official surveillance URL and the column name. For Canada it is PHAC's numtotal_last7. For the US it is CDC's new_cases. For the UK it is UKHSA's newCasesByPublishDate. Download it exactly as in Cell 2, just change the URL and column.

2

Set the population size in the config

Change pop_size_min = pop_size_max = YOUR_POPULATION. For a single country this is the national population. For a single city it is that city's population. The simulator scales everything proportionally, so the same code works for Iceland (370k) or India (1.4B).

3

Calibrate β to your disease

Look up the basic reproduction number R0 for your disease. R0 ≈ β/γ. If R0 is 2.5–3 (original COVID-19 strain) and γ ≈ 0.1, then β ≈ 0.25–0.30. For Omicron (R0 ≈ 8–15), β needs to be much higher — or you can capture Omicron-scale spread by widening beta_max to 0.40+. For seasonal flu (R0 ≈ 1.2–1.4), β ≈ 0.12–0.20. Widen the range by ±30% to cover variant uncertainty. Set these as beta_min and beta_max.

4

Tune the observation model to your surveillance system

Every country reports differently, and the observation model is only useful if it matches your actual surveillance system. Check your raw data first — plot the weekly totals and look for weekend dips, sudden jumps, and the under-reporting in early weeks. Then tune the four layers to match what you see. If your country had widespread testing from the start, set initial_rate high (0.5+). If early testing was near-zero, keep it low (0.05–0.2). Getting this layer right matters more than anything else in the observation model.

5

Choose T_IN and T_OUT for your use case

T_IN = 26 (6 months) works well for COVID-19 because wave dynamics unfold over months. For flu (shorter seasons) try T_IN = 12–16. T_OUT = 8 (2 months) is a practical public health horizon. For nowcasting (predicting today based on recent data) reduce T_OUT to 2–4 weeks.

6

Run rolling_forecast on your real series and evaluate

The rolling_forecast function requires zero changes — it operates on any numpy array of weekly counts. The rolling MAE it returns tells you how well the model calibrated to your data. Compare against a naive baseline (last-week repeat) and a simple seasonal model to contextualise the number.

10.2Tuning guide — what to change when results are poor

SymptomLikely causeWhat to change
Forecasts are always too lowObservation model under-corrects — simulated curves carry less noise than real dataReduce initial_rate in observation model; the model thinks more is visible than actually is
Forecasts miss the peak every timebeta_max too low — model never saw fast enough growthWiden beta_max to 0.45–0.50; increase wave_change_count_range upper bound
Uncertainty bands are too wideObservation noise too high in trainingTighten dow_stds and lab_acc variance in the observation model
Val loss much higher than train lossToo few trajectories — model memorised training windowsIncrease N_TRAIN to 30,000+; or add more window diversity by reducing STRIDE
Good on large provinces, poor on small onesScale mismatch in log-normalisationLog-normalise per-province separately; or train with a wider pop_size range
Model does not detect wave onset earlyT_IN too short for wave dynamicsIncrease T_IN to 52 weeks; switch to a Transformer backbone to handle longer context

10.3Extensions from the paper

ExtensionWhat to changeDifficulty
Forecast deaths as well as casesAdd a second output head; use numdeaths_last7 as a second target YLow
Province-specific modelsSet pop_size_min = pop_size_max = province_pop; run 10 separate configsLow
Longer horizon (12 or 16 weeks)Set T_OUT = 12; retrain; add more quantile levels (10th, 25th, 75th, 90th)Low
Nowcasting (predict current week)Set T_IN = 4–8, T_OUT = 1–2; remove delay layer from observation modelLow
Vaccine compartment (SVEAIR)Add V compartment with partial immunity; add waning vaccine protection parameterMedium
More training data (paper scale)Increase N_TRAIN to 100,000+; the original paper uses millions of trajectoriesMedium
Transformer backboneReplace encoder with a 4-layer causal Transformer for T_IN = 52+ week windowsMedium
Fine-tune on real dataAfter synthetic pre-training, run 3–5 epochs on held-out real data with LR=1e-4Medium
Foundation model (Mantis-style)Train on multiple diseases simultaneously; use a disease-embedding input layerHigh — see arXiv:2508.12260
✅ Reproducibility checklist — before you publish results

If you use this pipeline in a paper, thesis, or report, work through this list before citing numbers.

CheckPass conditionIf it fails
Val loss converged Val loss < 0.025 by epoch 10 Increase N_TRAIN to 30,000+ or train 5 more epochs
Beats naive baseline Rolling MAE < last-value MAE (repeating today’s count for all 8 future weeks) Check log-normalisation is applied at inference; retrain with wider β range
Interval coverage ~85–95% of actuals fall inside the 90% prediction band Observation model noise may be too narrow; widen and retrain
Parameter range covers target Target pathogen R0 falls within the simulator’s sampled β/γ range Recalibrate beta_max before training; do not apply zero-shot outside the trained range
Seed fixed Results reproducible across three independent runs with SEED = 42 Add torch.backends.cudnn.deterministic = True for GPU runs
No real data in training PHAC data used only at inference If you fine-tuned, report zero-shot and fine-tuned results separately

Optimising & improving your results

Default settings will give you a model that runs and produces forecasts. Whether those forecasts are good is a separate question. These are the settings that actually move the numbers, in rough order of impact:

O.1Adjusting hyperparameters

HyperparameterDefaultEffect of increasingEffect of decreasing
N_TRAIN15,000More trajectory diversity → lower val loss, better generalisationFaster to run; higher risk of memorising seen patterns
N_EPOCHS10Model sees each window more times; useful if loss has not plateauedFaster; use 3–5 for quick experiments
BATCH512Smoother gradient estimates; needs more GPU memoryNoisier updates; sometimes better generalisation
Initial lr1e-3Faster early convergence; risk of overshooting the optimumMore stable; slower; better for fine-tuning on real data
channels in EpiCNN64More expressive model; more parameters to trainFaster; may underfit on 15,000+ trajectories
T_IN26 weeksLonger context; captures slower wave dynamicsLess context; faster; suited to nowcasting

O.2Diagnosing overfitting vs. underfitting

After training, look at the val/train gap from Cell 14:

Val loss well above train loss

The model is memorising specific synthetic trajectories rather than learning general epidemic dynamics. This usually means N_TRAIN is too low. Try 30,000+ trajectories first, then add Dropout(0.2) after the Conv layers if it persists.

Both losses high

The model has not learned the epidemic patterns at all. First check for bugs — an observation model misconfiguration that produces all-zero outputs is a common culprit. If the data looks right, try more epochs or wider channels. Also check that your training data actually contains real variation in wave shape and timing.

O.3Transfer to real data: optional fine-tuning

The paper does not fine-tune on real data and still gets good results. But if you have real labelled data available — say, the last two years of PHAC case counts with actual future outcomes — a few epochs of fine-tuning can squeeze out extra performance. Use a very low learning rate (1e-4 or lower) to avoid overwriting what the model already learned:

Optional: fine-tune on real data
# Fine-tune with a lower learning rate to avoid overwriting the pre-trained weights
# Prepare a small real dataset: last N weeks of PHAC data
T_REAL = min(len(canada_obs) - T_IN - T_OUT, 60)   # up to 60 real training windows
X_real = []; Y_real = []
y_r = np.log1p(canada_obs); sc_r = y_r.max() + 1e-8; yn_r = y_r / sc_r
for s in range(0, T_REAL, 2):
    X_real.append(yn_r[s:s + T_IN]); Y_real.append(yn_r[s + T_IN:s + T_IN + T_OUT])

if X_real:
    X_r = torch.tensor(np.array(X_real, dtype=np.float32))
    Y_r = torch.tensor(np.array(Y_real, dtype=np.float32))
    ft_loader = DataLoader(TensorDataset(X_r, Y_r), batch_size=16, shuffle=True)
    ft_optim  = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=1e-4)  # 10x lower LR
    model.train()
    for epoch in range(3):   # just 3 epochs — don't overwrite pre-training
        for xb, yb in ft_loader:
            xb, yb = xb.to(DEVICE), yb.to(DEVICE)
            ft_optim.zero_grad()
            mean, lo, hi = model(xb)
            combined_loss(mean, lo, hi, yb).backward()
            torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 0.5)
            ft_optim.step()
        print(f'Fine-tune epoch {epoch+1}/3 done')
    print('Fine-tuning complete. Re-run rolling_forecast() to see improvement.')
📚 Further reading and citations

If this tutorial was useful in your work, please cite the original paper. A suggested blog citation follows.

BibTeX — Dudley et al. 2025 (the paper)
@article{dudley2025simulation,
  title   = {Simulation as Supervision: Mechanistic Pretraining
             for Scientific Discovery},
  author  = {Dudley, Carson and Magdaleno, Diana and
             Harding, Ethan and Eisenberg, Marisa},
  journal = {arXiv preprint arXiv:2507.08977},
  year    = {2025},
  url     = {https://arxiv.org/abs/2507.08977}
}
BibTeX — this tutorial (Canadian COVID-19 application)
@misc{anokye2025sgnn_canada,
  title  = {The Illustrated SGNN: Canadian COVID-19 Edition},
  author = {Anokye, Francis},
  year   = {2025},
  url    = {https://francisanokye.github.io/sgnn-canada/},
  note   = {Tutorial applying Simulation-Grounded Neural Networks
            to Canadian PHAC surveillance data}
}

For related work that contextualises the SGNN approach:

These cover every PyTorch concept that appears in the code above, from first principles:

  • PyTorch Official: Neural Networks — nn.Module, loss functions, backprop, weight update. The skeleton of Cell 12–13.
  • Sahin Ahmed: Train a NN in PyTorch — training loops, DataLoaders, evaluation, hyperparameter tuning. Directly parallels Cells 13–15.
  • PyTorch: Learn the Basics — tensors, transforms, optimisation, save/load. The complete foundation.
  • Dudley et al., 2025: Simulation as Supervision — the paper this blog implements.

The whole thing in five sentences

The counterintuitive part is worth sitting with: the model learned from simulations of diseases that never happened, in populations that do not exist, with surveillance systems that were procedurally generated. Then it forecasted real Canadian COVID-19 data. The provincial forecasts show how far that gets you — and also where it falls short. Smaller provinces with inconsistent reporting are harder. Early pandemic weeks with near-zero testing are harder. The method is not magic; it is a clever way to use what you already know.

The Illustrated SGNN: Canadian COVID-19 Edition · by Francis Anokye · based on Dudley et al., 2026 SGNN repo ↗  ·  site source ↗
